Get 24-Hour Exclusive Access to Shop Mango x Eckhaus Latta’s Summer Capsule Collection By Laura Jackson June 3, 2026 Photo: Courtesy of Mango Save Story Save this story Save Story Save this story “Yin and yang is built into this brand,” wrote Vogue Runway’s Nicole Phelps in her post-show review of Eckhaus Latta’s fall 2026 collection. Duality has long defined designers Mike Eckhaus and Zoe Latta, whose use of experimental materials, inclusive design, and commitment to creating clothes for real people has made the label a downtown-cool mainstay since its debut in 2011. Now, the brand is bringing its distinctive point of view to a wider audience with its first collaboration with the Spanish retailer Mango. A 43-piece capsule collection of sand-to-city staples, Mango x Eckhaus Latta is designed for both the city slicker and the seaside dweller, featuring gauzy dresses , signature lightweight knits, and even a selection of statement swimwear . With Vogue First, Vogue Shopping readers will get exclusive access to shop the drop 24 hours before anyone else. Arrow Arrow Following successful summer collaborations with labels like Siedrés and Simon Miller, it took little convincing when Mango first approached the design duo with the idea. “It was a win-win on both sides,” Zoe tells Vogue . “We also loved that they’re Mediterranean and have this innate summer ethos. Getting to design for this specific season and customer was really exciting.” On merging the two worlds, Mike explains, “We wanted it to be easy and fun, while still offering range for the individual. Taking into account that this could be someone’s first interaction with the brand, we didn’t want anything to feel too heavy-handed. Instead, we focused on keeping it distilled and accessible.” The collaboration also marks Eckhaus Latta’s first foray into a see-now-buy-now model—a departure from their traditional runway calendar. title: Why Is It So Hard to Find Good (Non Cash-Grab) Pride Merch? | Vogue
content: Why Is It So Hard to Find Good (Non Cash-Grab) Pride Merch? | Vogue Skip to main content Your All Access Pass GET THE APP Search Account Menu Menu Menu FASHION RUNWAY SHOPPING BEAUTY CULTURE LIVING VOGUE BUSINESS TEEN VOGUE FASHION Expand Celebrity Style Street Style Models Designers Trends Sustainability All Fashion RUNWAY SHOPPING Chevron BEAUTY Expand Celebrity Beauty Hair Makeup Nails Wellness Skin All Beauty CULTURE Expand Opinion TV & Movies Music Arts Books News Sports All Culture LIVING Expand Homes Travel Food Parties Royals All Living WEDDINGS Expand Wedding Inspiration Planning & Advice For Wedding Guests The Bridal Shop All Weddings VOGUE BUSINESS Expand Technology Sustainability Fashion Retail Companies Beauty Events Member Benefits Executive Reports VIDEO PHOTOVOGUE MORE NEWSLETTERS PODCAST MAGAZINE PROJECTS VOGUE ARCHIVE TEEN VOGUE Search Search Contact Us About Vogue Shopping Why Is It So Hard to Find Good (Non Cash-Grab) Pride Merch? By Christian Allaire June 10, 2026 Photo: Getty Images Save Story Save this story Save Story Save this story “ Hi, gay !” Nothing kicks off Pride Month quite like corporate rainbow-washing. Every June, mega-retailers get into the LGBTQ+ spirit by plastering rainbows and cheeky words like “slay!” onto colorful tees and hoodies. As a member of the community, I’m not hating on this approach per se : It’s the spirit of allyship that counts. Especially as our current administration continues to threaten LGBTQ+ health and rights across the nation, some pro-gay energy via fashion is harmless and, in fact, welcomed. But still: As someone who partakes in Pride Month but rarely wears flashy colors, I wonder why it’s so hard to find good Pride merch that isn’t a blatant cash-grab. Using the vague spirit of acceptance in a splashy way to tick an inclusivity box? Not very slay, if you ask me. Luckily, there are a few brands who have created Pride merch with intention this month—partnering with LGBTQ+ causes to use fashion for good. And better yet, in addition to the charitable components, the designs are even tasteful, too. Take New York designer Connor Ives and his signature “ Protect The Dolls ” tee, already worn by celebrities like Pedro Pascal and Troye Sivan. Not only does it have a clear message, but all of its proceeds are also donated directly to Trans Lifeline—a trans-led charity that delivers life-saving services to those who need them. Other chic and thoughtful Pride merch on the scene? Brands like David Yurman have released Pride-themed jewelry that benefits The Trevor Project, providing life-saving resources for LGBTQ+ youth. Levi’s has released denim items supporting Outright International, a global organization dedicated to advancing LGBTQ+ human rights. Turns out, you can authentically support the community, and wear more unexpected and discrete Pride merch. Not that there is anything wrong with the full-on rainbow approach—now is the month to be loud and proud. Just try not to buy the soulless co

title: In Honor of Father’s Day, 8 Treasures Vogue Staffers Inherited From Their Dads | Vogue
content: In Honor of Father’s Day, 8 Treasures Vogue Staffers Inherited From Their Dads | Vogue Skip to main content Your All Access Pass GET THE APP Search Account Menu Menu Menu FASHION RUNWAY SHOPPING BEAUTY CULTURE LIVING VOGUE BUSINESS TEEN VOGUE FASHION Expand Celebrity Style Street Style Models Designers Trends Sustainability All Fashion RUNWAY SHOPPING Chevron BEAUTY Expand Celebrity Beauty Hair Makeup Nails Wellness Skin All Beauty CULTURE Expand Opinion TV & Movies Music Arts Books News Sports All Culture LIVING Expand Homes Travel Food Parties Royals All Living WEDDINGS Expand Wedding Inspiration Planning & Advice For Wedding Guests The Bridal Shop All Weddings VOGUE BUSINESS Expand Technology Sustainability Fashion Retail Companies Beauty Events Member Benefits Executive Reports VIDEO PHOTOVOGUE MORE NEWSLETTERS PODCAST MAGAZINE PROJECTS VOGUE ARCHIVE TEEN VOGUE Search Search Contact Us About Vogue Culture The Treasures Vogue Staffers Inherited From Their Dads By Emma Specter June 21, 2026 Save Story Save this story Save Story Save this story We all inherit certain things from our parents, whether we like it or not. (I’m still allowed to blame my mom for my tendency toward messiness as an adult, right?) But sometimes the things we get from them aren’t just encoded in our DNA—if you’re lucky, you just might also have a few precious items from Mom and Dad that live on in your apartment. A handful of years ago, various designers spoke to Vogue about the beloved things they inherited from their mothers. Now, Vogue staffers are sharing the most emotionally resonant heirlooms they’ve been given—or, in certain cases, borrowed or straight-up stolen—from our fathers, from books to record collections to vests to taxidermy (!). Read about them all below: A favorite jacket My dad gave me (well, I stole it, but he let me) his amazing brown leather bomber jacket. It's perfectly worn-in and slouchy, and comes with a detachable shearling collar. I begged him for it as a teen and it wasn't until my mid-to-late 20s that he finally succumbed. It's one of my favorite jackets, for both sentimental and style reasons. —Christian Allaire, Senior Fashion & Style Writer A full classic-rock record collection I inherited my dad’s (dare I say) excellent music taste—particularly his love for the Rolling Stones. As a result, he passed down his entire teenage record collection to me! I started being interested in classic rock in my teens, and this brought us even closer together. My dad is a doctor, but briefly considered foregoing his medical career to instead be a drummer. Throughout college, he played in reggae and ska bands, and collected some great records. Pictured are some of his finds mixed with mine, including a copy of the Stones’ 1971 LP Sticky Fingers, which features artwork by Andy Warhol on the cover. This particular copy has the original functioning zipper of earlier pressings, which they stopped producing in 1977. —Fred Sahai, Associate Commerce Producer, Vog

title: How Biz Sherbert Scoured the Country for 50 Stylish Young Americans | Vogue
content: How Biz Sherbert Scoured the Country for 50 Stylish Young Americans | Vogue Skip to main content Your All Access Pass GET THE APP Search Account Menu Menu Menu FASHION RUNWAY SHOPPING BEAUTY CULTURE LIVING VOGUE BUSINESS TEEN VOGUE FASHION Expand Celebrity Style Street Style Models Designers Trends Sustainability All Fashion RUNWAY SHOPPING Chevron BEAUTY Expand Celebrity Beauty Hair Makeup Nails Wellness Skin All Beauty CULTURE Expand Opinion TV & Movies Music Arts Books News Sports All Culture LIVING Expand Homes Travel Food Parties Royals All Living WEDDINGS Expand Wedding Inspiration Planning & Advice For Wedding Guests The Bridal Shop All Weddings VOGUE BUSINESS Expand Technology Sustainability Fashion Retail Companies Beauty Events Member Benefits Executive Reports VIDEO PHOTOVOGUE MORE NEWSLETTERS PODCAST MAGAZINE PROJECTS VOGUE ARCHIVE TEEN VOGUE Search Search Contact Us About Vogue Fashion How Biz Sherbert Scoured the Country for 50 Stylish Young Americans By Biz Sherbert June 8, 2026 Photo: Avery Norman Save Story Save this story Save Story Save this story A few years ago, I started talking to people about how they dress. Some worked in fashion, but most were regular people—college students, baristas, shop clerks—getting dressed for their everyday lives. Speaking with them taught me more about style—how it shapes and reflects who we are and how we want to be seen—than anything I’d encountered in my career covering fashion and culture. These conversations eventually became my newsletter, American Style . I always gravitated toward young people. They’re the group everyone studies to determine the state and fate of culture—where it’s going, or if it’s totally lost—but who rarely get to represent themselves beyond their own content. When Vogue asked me to find 50 stylish people across the United States to mark its 250th anniversary, I knew I wanted to create a platform for young Americans in every corner of the country to share what style means to them. Applications came from everywhere, from Kentucky to Puerto Rico, and inspirations ranged from Georgia O’Keeffe and Pinterest to transcendental meditation and many, many mothers and grandmothers. After we narrowed down the 50 finalists, I traveled to Minnesota and Alabama to profile two of them: First stop, the suburbs of Minneapolis, where I met Owen Peters , 19, who is just as inspired by the frat boys at his university as he is by Jackie O. Then I was on to Birmingham, where I visited Chelsi Banks , also 19, who cut her teeth on fashion Twitter before developing her personal style during COVID. Choosing finalists for this project, I found myself inspired (albeit slightly overwhelmed) by page after page of spectacular outfits. Someone asked me recently how I pick who to feature in American Style . I told her it’s an almost ineffable mix of signals—I’m often drawn to outfits that feel considered, unexpected, or speak to something I’m already thinking about. But mostly it’s the sense that I’v

The Prime Day Clothing Deals I’m Shopping Before They Sell Out By Kristina Rutkowski June 26, 2026 Photo: Courtesy of Donni. Save Story Save this story Save Story Save this story It’s the final countdown! Today, Friday, June 26 marks the close on Prime Day 2026 , meaning it’s time to move on whatever’s in cart but not yet on its way to you. Since I’ve been covering Prime Day clothing deals for Vogue for a few years now, I can confirm the last day is where the best fashion markdowns tend to surface. My shopping masterplan always starts with wardrobe essentials , moves onto summer outfitting needs, and ends with last-minute vacation finds pieces for my upcoming summer trips. Masterfully minimal and endlessly versatile, it was a supermodel staple worn by the likes of Kate Moss and Naomi Campbell–and the very piece that inspired creative director Katie Kolodinski to launch her label, Silk Laundry, back in 2015. Kolodinski is equally passionate about sustainable practices, ensuring each item is crafted from natural, low-impact materials. It’s this blend of ethos–both fashion and function–that makes the brand such a style no-brainer: its pieces slip seamlessly into any wardrobe , pairing just as well with everyday denim as they do with polished tailoring or a party-ready pump. While the 90s Slip Dress remains the brand’s signature, Silk Laundry has since expanded into a full universe of sumptuous silk separates–from tailored shirting and fluid trousers to midiskirts and dresses in a range of refined silhouettes. Now, the label is unveiling its newest holiday drop: a 34-piece collection of festive finds in rich hues (burgundy, sage green, chocolate) and splashy prints (gingham, florals, botanicals) that work beautifully for every occasion, whether you’re going casual or dressing to the nines. This holiday season, save 15% on Silk Laundry’s best-selling satin staples when you shop the label as a Vogue Shopping Insider. Like all of Silk Laundry’s pieces, this latest collection is designed to be worn, loved, and revisited for years to come–heritage shopping at a discount, what could be better? Elsewhere, a crisp white jersey dress, a whisper-thin black slip from COS, and a striped knit from Me+Em prove that the simplest silhouettes are often the most compelling. Whether you're shopping for a wedding guest dress , a holiday, or merely the pleasure of a warm June evening, these are the pieces setting the tone for the season. However, we may earn affiliate revenue on this article and commission when you buy something. Long before the words quiet luxury were ever uttered, The Row established itself as the archetype of anonymous chic. When Ashley and Mary-Kate Olsen launched their brand The Row in 2006, the goal was to create the perfect T-shirt. It’s safe to say that the mission has been accomplished and then some. The Row has become synonymous with effortless, wearable luxury. The Row Handbag Checklist The Marlo The Row Marlo 12 bag Read more $4,900 THE ROW The Peggy The Row Peggy clutch Read more $2,900 THE ROW The Park The Row small N/S Park tote edge bag Read more $1,500 THE ROW The ’90s Bag The Row 90's bag Read more $1,090 THE ROW The Izzy The Row N/S Izzy bag Read more $2,300 THE ROW The Alger The Row Alger leather bag Read more $4,650 NET-A-PORTER The India The Row India 12.00 bag Read more $3,890 THE ROW The Marcel The Row Marcel tote bag Read more $3,200 NET-A-PORTER In fact, the name of the label itself is rooted in sartorial excellence, paying homage to London’s high-class Savile Row, the British road known for producing impeccably tailored men’s suiting and exceptional fabrics. Over the years, that same attention to detail, married with a love of simple silhouettes, has carried over from clothing and into The Row’s coveted collection of handbags . From the ’90s bag to the Bourse, many of The Row’s luxurious styles offer a no-fuss attitude that we’ve come to love from the brand. Ahead, we take a deep dive into all of the need-to-know silhouettes to add to your closet. The Marlo Meet the Marlo, The Row’s slouchier answer to the (no-longer-in-stock!) Marga

title: Tommy’s Take on the Joy of Accessorizing | Vogue
content: Tommy’s Take on the Joy of Accessorizing | Vogue Skip to main content Your All Access Pass GET THE APP Search Account Menu Menu Menu FASHION RUNWAY SHOPPING BEAUTY CULTURE LIVING VOGUE BUSINESS TEEN VOGUE New Arrivals Summer in the City The Vacation Shop Under $300 Shoes Bags Editor Wish Lists FASHION Expand Celebrity Style Street Style Models Designers Trends Sustainability All Fashion RUNWAY SHOPPING Chevron BEAUTY Expand Celebrity Beauty Hair Makeup Nails Wellness Skin All Beauty CULTURE Expand Opinion TV & Movies Music Arts Books News Sports All Culture LIVING Expand Homes Travel Food Parties Royals All Living WEDDINGS Expand Wedding Inspiration Planning & Advice For Wedding Guests The Bridal Shop All Weddings VOGUE BUSINESS Expand Technology Sustainability Fashion Retail Companies Beauty Events Member Benefits Executive Reports VIDEO PHOTOVOGUE MORE NEWSLETTERS PODCAST MAGAZINE PROJECTS VOGUE ARCHIVE TEEN VOGUE Search Search Contact Us About Vogue Fashion Tommy’s Take on the Joy of Accessorizing By Tommy Ton June 21, 2026 Save Story Save this story Save Story Save this story Tommy Ton has always had a unique point of view on what makes an outfit sing. One of fashion’s earliest street style photographers, his subjects—individuals with style and substance in equal measure—aren't following trends. They’re expressing something deeper, and that difference is what makes his shots so compelling. Sure, the clothes are always great, but his takes capture the true essence of the wearer. In monthly installments surrounding a fashion theme, he'll explore the outfits that caught his eye and why. Here’s Tommy’s Take. Fashion experts have always emphasized investing in a wardrobe of foundational essentials: the perfect white shirt, a tailored suit, a little black dress, and so on. But once you’ve found those building blocks, the next question becomes: how do you avoid getting caught up in the constant seasonal cycle of trends just to make your closet feel relevant again? The answer is quite simple: focus on accessories. Small accents, whether it’s a bag in an interesting color, a brooch that has a deeper meaning, or a belt that invites a closer look, can instantly give your wardrobe more clarity and a fresh perspective. Clothes come and go, but a beautifully curated collection of accessories stays in rotation for longer than a single season when you invest wisely in the right pieces. After all, no one ever said you can have too many accessories. The laidback shoe has cemented its status over the years as a warm-weather essential for celebrity devotees and fashion editors alike. Probably the most well known in the category is the humble Birkenstock Arizona, which received a major rebrand when Kate Moss wore a pair in her breakout cover shoot for The Face in 1990. Soon after, Marc Jacobs famously featured the double-buckle Arizona sandal throughout his now-infamous 1993 grunge collection for Perry Ellis , instantly transforming the hippie style into a fashion staple—making wearable and comfortable sandals feel undeniably cool. Phoebe Philo debuted a pair of strapped fur-lined shoes in 2013 during her tenure at Céline, a riff on the beloved “ugly-chic” sandals that reignited the shoe’s relevancy. title: How to Style a Button-Up Shirt This Summer–7 Foolproof Outfit Ideas | Vogue

Part of a solid rotation of wardrobe essentials , this menswear-inspired It item has garnered a cult-like following that includes supermodels, from Kendall Jenner and Gigi Hadid, to our favorite celebrities like Katie Holmes. Like any closet classic, it’s likely that you already own a button-up or two. Now comes the fun part (and where we come in): figuring out how to style it, especially for the warmer months. Vogue’s Go-To Button-Ups: The Wide Stripe H&M oversized shirt Jump to review $40 H&M The Crisp White Banana Republic The Oversized shirt Jump to review $98 BANANA REPUBLIC The Breezy Linen J.Crew garçon classic shirt Jump to review $118 J.CREW The Checked Choice Toteme checked cotton shirt Jump to review $720 NET-A-PORTER The Chic Black Gap cotton poplin big shirt Jump to review $70 GAP The Narrow Stripe Leset Yoshi striped shirt Jump to review $260 NET-A-PORTER In building your collection, a bright white poplin is essential. Comme Si’s La Classica shirt gets the fabric and cut just right, and has been a longtime editor favorite. A bold blue button-up is another minimalist must-have that adds a subtle pop of color to your look. I have been loving linen styles for summer, effortlessly thrown over a bathing suit or tucked into white jeans for the office . Styling wise, let the shirt have a moment—think halfway buttoned with a statement necklace peeking out, or wrapped around your waist for a finishing touch. When it comes to the classics, you can never go wrong with a simple stripe (wide or narrow). I love Almina Concept’s oversized blue and white iteration worn casually over a statement skirt . Meanwhile, Matteau’s brown and white shirt is the perfect alternative to traditional white and black stripe

title: How to Save 15% on Studio Amelia’s Seriously Sleek FW25 Collection | Vogue
content: How to Save 15% on Studio Amelia’s Seriously Sleek FW25 Collection | Vogue Skip to main content Your All Access Pass GET THE APP Search Account Menu Menu Menu FASHION RUNWAY SHOPPING BEAUTY CULTURE LIVING VOGUE BUSINESS TEEN VOGUE New Arrivals Summer in the City The Vacation Shop Under $300 Shoes Bags Editor Wish Lists FASHION Expand Celebrity Style Street Style Models Designers Trends Sustainability All Fashion RUNWAY SHOPPING Chevron BEAUTY Expand Celebrity Beauty Hair Makeup Nails Wellness Skin All Beauty CULTURE Expand Opinion TV & Movies Music Arts Books News Sports All Culture LIVING Expand Homes Travel Food Parties Royals All Living WEDDINGS Expand Wedding Inspiration Planning & Advice For Wedding Guests The Bridal Shop All Weddings VOGUE BUSINESS Expand Technology Sustainability Fashion Retail Companies Beauty Events Member Benefits Executive Reports VIDEO PHOTOVOGUE MORE NEWSLETTERS PODCAST MAGAZINE PROJECTS VOGUE ARCHIVE TEEN VOGUE Search Search Contact Us About Vogue Offers How to Save 15% on Studio Amelia’s Seriously Sleek FW25 Collection By Laura Jackson November 3, 2025 Save Story Save this story Save Story Save this story Photo: Courtesy of Studio Amelia Aussie-based label Studio Amelia isn’t just an editor favorite—it’s also earned the celebrity and street-style seal of approval. You’ve likely spotted its signature Cross Front 70 heeled sandals everywhere from the streets of SoHo to the Met Gala, where Kendall Jenner paired a custom satin version with her glittering sequin Givenchy gown in 2024. While the barely-there sandal remains the brand’s calling card—it’s what put Studio Amelia on the map, after all—the label has since evolved beautifully. Their FW25 collection proves it: elegant fashion pumps in both python-print and fire-engine red, scrunched-leather kitten heels that rework a classic silhouette, and stiletto ankle boots in rich chocolate brown and deep onyx that bring instant refinement to any look. Style their gold or ivory Odessa sandals with a dazzling party dress this holiday season, or opt for the pointed-toe Vivionne pumps and a pleated midi skirt for an effortless commute-to-cocktail transition. Complete any outfit with one of their newest handbags—the top-stitched mini tote in classic black or the Zamora python clutch are total conversation starters. Beyond the design appeal, Studio Amelia’s ethos is rooted in craft and consciousness. Each piece is produced through sustainable manufacturing practices, with an emphasis on timeless design and attainable luxury. Consider its standout styles the quiet centerpieces of your wardrobe—polished, versatile, and always just the right amount of statement. As an Vogue Shopping Insider, shop and save 15% on their most sought-after silhouettes from the new FW25 collection below. Your closet will thank you. Shop Our Favorite Celebrity Looks of the Week By Kristina Rutkowski June 26, 2026 Photo: Courtesy of Backgrid, Raymond Hall, Dave Benett/Getty Images, Neil Mockford Save Story Save this story Save Story Save this story Summer is in full swing and with the uptick in temperatures comes the need for stylish wardrobe inspiration. We’re here to simplify matters with warm-weather (ahem, heatwave-proof) outfit ideas , courtesy of our favorite celebrities spotted on the streets . Consider this week’s edit your guide to everything from summer-in-the-city dressing to vacation-ready looks, infused with plenty of off-duty flair. Vogue ’ s Favorite Celeb-Inspired Finds of the Week: The Button-Down Shirt J.Crew garçon classic shirt Jump to review $118 J.CREW The Slip Dress Leset Barb midi dress Jump to review $320 NET-A-PORTER The Leather Loafers Jamie Haller penny loafers Jump to review $625 SHOPBOP The Layered Tee Guest in Residence Layer T-shirt Jump to review $265 NET-A-PORTER The Everyday Pants La Ligne Colby pants Jump to review $325 SHOPBOP The Striped Sweater Loewe sleeveless sweater Jump to review $950 LOEWE The Lace-Trim Skirt Dôen Elowette skirt Jump to review $350 NET-A-PORTER The Cap-Toe Flats Margaux The Cap-Toe Demi flats Jump to review $295 NORDSTROM With all-time high temperatures recorded around the world, focusing on lightweight summer staples is essential. Jennifer Lawrence went with easy-breezy oversized shirting and flowy pants from La Ligne for a bike ride in New York City, while Kylie Jenner chose an all-black ensemble for a walk with Timothée Chalamet, consisting of a tank top, cropped trouser pants , and Maison Margiela Tabi ballet flats . As summer travel kicks into high gear, rely on Gigi Hadid’s stylish airport outfit for inspiration ahead of the long weekend . The supermodel wore a layered cashmere T-shirt from her brand Guest in Residence, styling it with breezy poplin pants and suede loafers for the ideal mix of comfort and polish. Not in of

Personality and practicality should go hand in hand. Luckily, the season’s hottest handbag trends deliver both in spades. Much of the excitement can be traced back to the designer debuts unveiled at several major houses earlier this year, from Matthieu Blazy at Chanel to Jonathan Anderson at Dior. Both creatives looked to the archives for inspiration—Blazy reimagined Chanel’s iconic 2.55 bags, reinforcing them with wire to create subtly warped silhouettes, while Anderson unveiled his own take on the iconic Lady Dior. But they’re not alone: at Balenciaga, Pierpaolo Piccioli updated the Le City, while at Celine, Michael Rider brought back Phoebe Philo’s Phantom bag, complete with curved zips that leaned into its “smiley face bag” nickname. The Key Summer 2026 Handbag Trends: The Raffia Bag Hunting Season The Small Trapeze tote Jump to review $495 SHOPBOP The Canvas Tote J.Crew 1998 Montauk tote Jump to review $148 J.CREW The Sleek Satin The Row Cecily bag Jump to review $1,250 THE ROW The Beaded Bag Staud Baja mini tote Jump to review $295 NORDSTROM The Netted Shopper Alaïa Hip large bucket bag Jump to review $3,760 MYTHERESA The New Ladylike Balenciaga Rodeo handbag Jump to review $3,300 BALENCIAGA The Notable Navy Jil Sander Goji Pillow shoulder bag Jump to review $3,150 NET-A-PORTER The Summer Clutch Khaite Blake clutch Jump to review $3,200 KHAITE With a strong foundation set, more seasonal styles have since emerged. We’re seeing an array of elaborate netted bags at Tory Burch, Jil Sander, and Erdem—all of which make for a chic alternative to summer’s standby raffia bag. For those wanting the real thing, though, textured raffia styles from The Row and Alaïa are just as city-friendly as they are pool-ready. Classic canvas totes from Kallmeyer, Toteme, and Staud deserve similar airtime, providing cool finishing touches to everything from w

This season marked a true industry reset, with nearly 15 newly appointed creative directors debuting their visions and setting the tone for the spring/summer 2026 fashion trends across the major fashion houses. The overarching sentiment was expressive, celebratory, and deeply personal—driven by individuality and the pure joy of getting dressed. The Top Summer 2026 Fashion Trends The Playful Set Faithfull Sable Playa set Jump to review $230 NET-A-PORTER The Cargo Pant Slvrlake Tess pants Jump to review $390 NET-A-PORTER The Solid One-Piece Lido Sedici swimsuit Jump to review $190 NET-A-PORTER The Button-Down Buck Mason Officer shirt Jump to review $288 BUCK MASON The Sporty Pick Saint Laurent cupro shorts Jump to review $1,550 SAINT LAURENT The Scarf Top Mango tie-neck top Jump to review $180 NORDSTROM The Jazz Age Dress Cos sculpted midi dress Jump to review $129 COS The Fringe Maxi Loulou de Saison Tiwa maxi dress Jump to review $390 NET-A-PORTER For summer, designers toyed with the idea of hobby dressing. We saw bike helmets at Celine, aprons at Miu Miu, and board shorts at Dries Van Noten. A sense of proportion play also came through with billowy balloon trousers, Bermuda shorts, and peplum tops. A certain kind of soft, relaxed elegance was also explored this season, characterized by scarf styling and drop-waist silhouettes. Elsewhere, bold retro prints brought nostalgic charm to the collections; from Chloé’s ’80s-inspired florals to Khaite’s oversized polka dots, these colors and patterns are what make summer dressing so fun and intoxicating. The spring/summer 2026 collections pick up where we left off in the fall, with personality-driven look

title: How to Style Loafers for Summer, Whether Your Look Is Playful or Polished | Vogue

Maybe you’re flip-flop loyalist, maybe you’re introducing something new—like a wedge sandal . But there’s something to be said about keeping those walkable classics from the transitional period in rotation, like the soft ballet or loafer , both of which embody that low-effort, high-reward sense of style we all strive for. Vogue’s Guide to Styling Loafers for Summer The Soft Loafer The Row Canal loafers Jump to review $990 THE ROW The Au Natural Le Monde Béryl woven leather loafers Jump to review $620 NET-A-PORTER The Classic Suede Reformation Francine ruched loafers Jump to review $298 SHOPBOP The Menswear-Inspired G.H.Bass Whitney Weejuns penny loafers Jump to review $195 NORDSTROM The Penny Loafer Jamie Haller leather penny loafers Jump to review $770 NET-A-PORTER The Simple and Sleek Khaite Miles loafers Jump to review $1,080 KHAITE The Boat Shoe Prada Shuffle antiqued boat shoes Jump to review $1,170 PRADA The Runway-Approved Pick Saint Laurent Le Loafers Jump to review $1,200 SAINT LAURENT It’s likely that you already have a pair of loafers stashed somewhere in your closet—which brings us to our next point: how to style them for summer. Whether your loafer of choice is classic, or more seasonal (say, a raffia style or a soft white deck shoe), let our guide below serve as your starting point for how wear them right now. You’ll quickly find them to just as versatile for warm weather as cooler days—suitable for nearly any kind of scenario, from the office to the weekend and anywhere you end up in between. They’re a natural match for linen trousers, and contrast Bermuda shorts nicely, but they also play well with mini dresses and midi ski

title: 20 Fashion Girl-Approved Wedding Brands to Shop for Your Big Day | Vogue
content: 20 Fashion Girl-Approved Wedding Brands to Shop for Your Big Day | Vogue Skip to main content Your All Access Pass GET THE APP Search Account Menu Menu Menu FASHION RUNWAY SHOPPING BEAUTY CULTURE LIVING VOGUE BUSINESS TEEN VOGUE New Arrivals Summer in the City The Vacation Shop Under $300 Shoes Bags Editor Wish Lists FASHION Expand Celebrity Style Street Style Models Designers Trends Sustainability All Fashion RUNWAY SHOPPING Chevron BEAUTY Expand Celebrity Beauty Hair Makeup Nails Wellness Skin All Beauty CULTURE Expand Opinion TV & Movies Music Arts Books News Sports All Culture LIVING Expand Homes Travel Food Parties Royals All Living WEDDINGS Expand Wedding Inspiration Planning & Advice For Wedding Guests The Bridal Shop All Weddings VOGUE BUSINESS Expand Technology Sustainability Fashion Retail Companies Beauty Events Member Benefits Executive Reports VIDEO PHOTOVOGUE MORE NEWSLETTERS PODCAST MAGAZINE PROJECTS VOGUE ARCHIVE TEEN VOGUE Search Search Contact Us About Vogue Wedding These Are the Fashion Girl-Approved Brands to Shop for Your Wedding By Anny Choi and Alexis Ventura June 9, 2026 Photos: Courtesy of Róhe, Wiederhoeft and Danielle Frankel Save Story Save this story Save Story Save this story With peak summer wedding season upon us, many brands that may at first read as non-bridal are putting their own twist on what occasion wear should look like. Skip the princess gowns and the chiffon tulle; experimental silhouettes and artfully detailed pieces feel far more exciting these days. If this sounds like your style, you’re in luck: between non-conventional wedding dresses from the likes of Wiederhoeft and Colleen Allen and It-girl accessories from Sandy Liang and Simone Rocha (and, of course, plenty of fancy separates to dress up in in the lead-up to the big day), there’s no shortage of cool-girl bridal brands reimagining tradition. Choose a statement-making, genderless two-piece set by Tanner Fletcher or commit to a whimsical look from Batsheva’s first-ever bridal collection. There are no parameters for what a bridal gown should look like; after taking stock of the plethora of options available, you may just find that you don’t actually want to shop from a wedding designer at all. Ahead, 20 fashion girl-approved designers to get to know as you plan your special day. Colleen Allen Courtesy of Colleen Allen Colleen Allen is one of the most exciting young designers in New York. After debuting a custom wedding dress for a Vogue bride last summer , she’s chosen to integrate more bridal-inspired pieces into her collections. Her references may be rooted in the past, but her designs feel modern and can be worn with ease. She incorporates small details and expert tailoring into all of her work that even your most discerning bridesmaid will love; like the effortless draping of the silk-duchesse coat, the perfect layer for a winter city wedding . title: Feels Like a Steal: Shop Vogue Favorite Under-$300 Finds | Vogue
content: Feels Like a Steal: Shop Vogue Favorite Under-$300 Finds | Vogue Skip to main content Your All Access Pass GET THE APP Search Account Menu Menu Menu FASHION RUNWAY SHOPPING BEAUTY CULTURE LIVING VOGUE BUSINESS TEEN VOGUE New Arrivals Summer in the City The Vacation Shop Under $300 Shoes Bags Editor Wish Lists FASHION Expand Celebrity Style Street Style Models Designers Trends Sustainability All Fashion RUNWAY SHOPPING Chevron BEAUTY Expand Celebrity Beauty Hair Makeup Nails Wellness Skin All Beauty CULTURE Expand Opinion TV & Movies Music Arts Books News Sports All Culture LIVING Expand Homes Travel Food Parties Royals All Living WEDDINGS Expand Wedding Inspiration Planning & Advice For Wedding Guests The Bridal Shop All Weddings VOGUE BUSINESS Expand Technology Sustainability Fashion Retail Companies Beauty Events Member Benefits Executive Reports VIDEO PHOTOVOGUE MORE NEWSLETTERS PODCAST MAGAZINE PROJECTS VOGUE ARCHIVE TEEN VOGUE Search Search Contact Us About Vogue Fashion Feels Like a Steal: Julietta’s Beaded Necklaces By Vogue June 24, 2026 Save Story Save this story Save Story Save this story Love a good fashion find? Same. And while there’s plenty we recommend on a daily basis, what makes us play for keeps is in the name: when it feels like a steal. Anything is fair game, as long as it’s under $300. Think of it as a direct line into our wardrobes, with the fashion math to back it up. Expect new names and upcoming brands—spotlighted weekly by our editors. Julietta’s Beaded Necklaces When summer emerges, so do a flurry of summer jewelry brands who have a distinct point of view and add a touch of personality and uniqueness to your wardrobe. Julietta is one of those labels—they manage to be something to everyone without loosing its identity; from onyx necklaces, statement shells and this turquoise stone necklace, its pieces are proof a small statement goes along way. And with most of its pieces ringing in under $300, you’ll easily get your price per wear. — Libby Jane Page, global executive shopping director Save to wishlist Save to wishlist Julietta Donna beaded necklace $245 NET-A-PORTER Leset Arielle City Shorts I never thought I could pull off Bermuda shorts at 5’2”—in my mind, you had to be tall to wear them. And so, for as long as I can remember, I’ve steered clear of anything knee-length; there was no happy medium between mini or midi. What drew me to Leset’s black Bermuda shorts one recent Saturday in the West Village I can’t quite say; likely, a mix of joyous optimism and the chance desire to step out of my comfort zone and try something unexpected. I loved everything about them from the moment I put them on: the effortless pull-on silhouette, the seamless crepe fabric, and, to my greatest surprise, the length. They hit me just below the knee when worn on the natural waist, and since they don’t have pleats, flare out in the most flattering way. I’ve lost track of how many times I’ve worn them in the weeks since buying them—I’m only ha

The homespun staple still shines poolside, of course, but we’re particularly drawn to the way fashion insiders are styling the silhouette in the city —with sleek sandals, elevated accessories, and a welcome dose of polish. Vogue ’s Guide to Styling Crochet Dresses for Summer The Colorful Stripe La DoubleJ scalloped crocheted maxi dress Jump to review $690 NET-A-PORTER The Runway It Item Diotima trio web crochet dress Jump to review $995 BERGDORF GOODMAN The Tank Dress Rabanne embellished crochet midi dress Jump to review $2,490 BERGDORF GOODMAN The Beach Cover-up Suzie Kondi Kristina crocheted mini dress Jump to review $695 NET-A-PORTER The Vacation Dress Alémais Dolly crochet dress Jump to review $690 NET-A-PORTER The Festive LBD Maria McManus pom pom macrame dress Jump to review $2,790 MARIA MCMANUS The Crochet Caftan Miguelina Arelis maxi dress Jump to review $575 NET-A-PORTER The Pretty Mini Dôen Remiette dress Jump to review $998 DÔEN A warm-weather favorite year after year, crochet’s enduring appeal was reflected on the spring 2026 runways , where designers like Gabriela Hearst, Diotima, and Maria McManus embraced the handcrafted technique. And while crochet tops and skirts deserve their own cart attention, it’s the dress that offers the most versatility—just as easy to zhuzh up or down. Alongside those runway names, contemporary labels like La DoubleJ, Alémais, and Suzie Kondi are similarly hooked on crochet dresses. title: How to Save 20% on Bevza’s Modern Minimalist Designs | Vogue
content: How to Save 20% on Bevza’s Modern Minimalist Designs | Vogue Skip to main content Your All Access Pass GET THE APP Search Account Menu Menu Menu FASHION RUNWAY SHOPPING BEAUTY CULTURE LIVING VOGUE BUSINESS TEEN VOGUE New Arrivals Summer in the City The Vacation Shop Under $300 Shoes Bags Editor Wish Lists FASHION Expand Celebrity Style Street Style Models Designers Trends Sustainability All Fashion RUNWAY SHOPPING Chevron BEAUTY Expand Celebrity Beauty Hair Makeup Nails Wellness Skin All Beauty CULTURE Expand Opinion TV & Movies Music Arts Books News Sports All Culture LIVING Expand Homes Travel Food Parties Royals All Living WEDDINGS Expand Wedding Inspiration Planning & Advice For Wedding Guests The Bridal Shop All Weddings VOGUE BUSINESS Expand Technology Sustainability Fashion Retail Companies Beauty Events Member Benefits Executive Reports VIDEO PHOTOVOGUE MORE NEWSLETTERS PODCAST MAGAZINE PROJECTS VOGUE ARCHIVE TEEN VOGUE Search Search Contact Us About Vogue Offers How to Save 20% on Bevza’s Modern Minimalist Designs By Vogue October 1, 2025 Save Story Save this story Save Story Save this story Photo: Courtesy of Bevza Established in Kyiv in 2006, Bevza’s mission is to celebrate Ukraine's heritage through a modern, minimalistic lens. Championing traditional artisanal techniques whilst taking cues from national symbols and folk songs, each collection is cultured, cool, and classic. Bevza’s modern designs eschew passing trends in favor of endurance. Each piece adds to the foundation of a wear-forever, love-forever closet—with the craft credentials to back the intended longevity up. Pamela Anderson, Bella Hadid, and Emily Ratajkowski are noted fans. One of Bevza’s signature hues is white—something you might already know if you follow the brand’s ultra-chic founder, Svitlana Bevza , on Instagram. A standout design from the fall 2025 collection is the Fiona maxi dress that’s softly gathered into a clean-lined but pretty silhouette that channels the season’s ladylike mood. Layer the low neckline with a long leather pendant (or one of the label’s vintage-style mirrors ) and add flats for a masculin féminin vibe. We also love the white bra sweater that’s elevated by lingerie detailing. Similar to Georgia O’Keeffe, who deeply engaged with white but wasn’t exclusively focused on the color, Bevza plays with black, gray, and refined pops of zing. For the new season , there’s a timeless black coat influenced by the warmth and elegance of a kozhukh—a Ukrainian winter jacket traditionally worn by men in the 19th century—and in the gray area, there’s a relaxed take on the fall-favorite pencil skirt sliced with slits. Team both with loafers . The fall ‘zing’ comes from the Spike dress in chilli-pepper red—a hot hue right now. Lastly, if you’re in the market for some outfit plus-ones, Bevza has options aplenty. Our favorite? The hand-knitted flat cap that’s produced with zero-waste technology. Hats are one of this season’s defining accessories , after all. D

Both minimalists and maximalists, and quite frankly everyone in between can find joy and inspiration from the collections, be it a newfound eclecticism, a novel approach to layering, or a quirky accessory addition; the clothes weren’t the only thing that was interesting this season, but how they were worn, too. Featured in this article The Monochrome Cord Nili Lotan Florence cotton-blend corduroy flared pants Jump to review $450 NET-A-PORTER The Modern Prep Polo Polo Ralph Lauren oversize button-up shirt Jump to review $168 NORDSTROM The Boho Skirt Dries Van Noten print midi skirt Jump to review $1,225 NORDSTROM The Leather Investment Nour Hammour leather barrel-leg pants See More $1,200 NET-A-PORTER The Sequin Top Prada embroidered sequin top See More $3,700 PRADA The Pop of Red Róhe wool-blend sweater Jump to review $585 MYTHERESA The Country-Coded Burberry check wool midi kilt See More $2,095 BURBERRY The Funnel-Neck Jacket J.Crew standing collar blazer Jump to review $268 J.CREW A wave of whip, or should we say book-smart styling tricks came from an assortment of prep-inspired looks, giving us fresh ways to wear and rewear those classic heritage pieces with an individual twist—layered button-downs, sharp pencil skirts, relaxed varsity jackets, all to be bookmarked for later. Then, there was a farewell to ultra-quiet luxury. Even the most quintessential minimalist or wardrobing-centric brands couldn’t resist incorporating texture via tangible elements such as fringe, tassels, or extreme draping. And of course, a feminine spirit infused collections from Chloé to Dries Van Noten, transcending sophomoric notions of boho . Here, a first (shoppable!) look at the key fall 2025 fashion tr

title: The Top Fall 2025 Color Trends, From Powder Pink to Mocha Brown | Vogue
content: The Top Fall 2025 Color Trends, From Powder Pink to Mocha Brown | Vogue Skip to main content Your All Access Pass GET THE APP Search Account Menu Menu Menu FASHION RUNWAY SHOPPING BEAUTY CULTURE LIVING VOGUE BUSINESS TEEN VOGUE New Arrivals Summer in the City The Vacation Shop Under $300 Shoes Bags Editor Wish Lists FASHION Expand Celebrity Style Street Style Models Designers Trends Sustainability All Fashion RUNWAY SHOPPING Chevron BEAUTY Expand Celebrity Beauty Hair Makeup Nails Wellness Skin All Beauty CULTURE Expand Opinion TV & Movies Music Arts Books News Sports All Culture LIVING Expand Homes Travel Food Parties Royals All Living WEDDINGS Expand Wedding Inspiration Planning & Advice For Wedding Guests The Bridal Shop All Weddings VOGUE BUSINESS Expand Technology Sustainability Fashion Retail Companies Beauty Events Member Benefits Executive Reports VIDEO PHOTOVOGUE MORE NEWSLETTERS PODCAST MAGAZINE PROJECTS VOGUE ARCHIVE TEEN VOGUE Search Search Contact Us About Vogue Fashion The Top Fall 2025 Color Trends, From Powder Pink to Mocha Brown By Cortne Bonilla August 12, 2025 Photo, Vogue Runway: Carven, Tory Burch, Bally, Ferragamo, Prada, Dries Van Noten Save Story Save this story Save Story Save this story Naturally, as the days grow shorter and the air turns crisper, the season’s color trends invite a richer, more decadent palette into our wardrobes, often stemming directly from the runways. The fall/winter 2025 color trends capture the season’s duality of bold self-expression and cozy warmth. Featured in this article The Powder Pink Shirt Everlane The Boyfriend shirt Jump to review $118 EVERLANE The Red Hot Pants Alex Mill coco sport pants Jump to review $168 ALEX MILL The Chocolate Shoulder Bag Gucci Jackie Giorno shoulder bag Jump to review $4,900 MYTHERESA The Blue Sheer Skirt Cos sheer column skirt Jump to review $149 COS The Slime Green Mule Staud Brigitte mule sandals Jump to review $295 BLOOMINGDALE'S The Electric Purple Top Khaite Keefe top Jump to review $1,080 KHAITE But it’s not all net-new: several colors from spring/summer 2025 are still going strong. The wispy, powdery blush that evolved from 2023’s Barbie pink remains relevant for fall. A rich, chocolate-y brown (coined by Pantone as “Mocha Mousse”) also maintains a strong presence this season with many designers, from Fendi to Saint Laurent, opting for tonal suiting and unexpected pairings—alongside pale greens and purples seen at Prada and Gucci. Siren red holds fast, popping up in full looks at Fforme and Acne Studios, but also as striking flashes at Ferragamo and Tod’s. Even those who prefer to abstain from color can still find ways to incorporate trending hues into their day-to-day rotations. Slime green works as a neutral in itself, and the same can be said for the sea of blues dominating the collections—from pastels to cobalt—which work easily with whites, creams, beiges, and browns. title: The Vogue Runway Fall 2025 Accessory Report: See the 183 Hats, Shoes, Bags, and More That Define the Fall 2025 Season | Vogue
content: The Vogue Runway Fall 2025 Accessory Report: See the 183 Hats, Shoes, Bags, and More That Define the Fall 2025 Season | Vogue Skip to main content Your All Access Pass GET THE APP Search Account Menu Menu Menu FASHION RUNWAY SHOPPING BEAUTY CULTURE LIVING VOGUE BUSINESS TEEN VOGUE FASHION Expand Celebrity Style Street Style Models Designers Trends Sustainability All Fashion RUNWAY SHOPPING Chevron BEAUTY Expand Celebrity Beauty Hair Makeup Nails Wellness Skin All Beauty CULTURE Expand Opinion TV & Movies Music Arts Books News Sports All Culture LIVING Expand Homes Travel Food Parties Royals All Living WEDDINGS Expand Wedding Inspiration Planning & Advice For Wedding Guests The Bridal Shop All Weddings VOGUE BUSINESS Expand Technology Sustainability Fashion Retail Companies Beauty Events Member Benefits Executive Reports VIDEO PHOTOVOGUE MORE NEWSLETTERS PODCAST MAGAZINE PROJECTS VOGUE ARCHIVE TEEN VOGUE Search Search Contact Us About Vogue Runway The Vogue Runway Fall 2025 Accessory Report: See the 183 Hats, Shoes, Bags, and More That Define the Fall 2025 Season By Laia Garcia-Furtado March 19, 2025 Save Story Save this story Save Story Save this story It was a curious season, fall 2025. Against an unstable, precarious world, designers forged ahead making clothes for future versions of us—six months into the future, but into the future nonetheless. On the runways two themes emerged: a return to a classical femininity and a call for individuality and the end of cookie-cutter, formula dressing. Are these two ideas on opposite ends of the spectrum? Almost certainly. And, yet, this season they easily became one. See, for example, the return of the eccentric little hat spotted everywhere from the stalwarts of elegance and luxury like Giorgio Armani and Loro Piana to the madcap visions of Anna Sui and Moschino ’s Adrian Appiolaza . Nothing says “we used to live in a society” like watching a classic Hollywood film or coming across vintage photos of women out in the world wearing their hats, a most important sign of propriety. Now, chapeaux communicate a certain artiness—the opposite of falling back, fitting in, and living your life the way the powers that be might tell you that you should. As my colleague Laird Borrelli-Persson explained in her ready-to-wear trend report , the hourglass silhouette—whether god-given, man-made, or sartorially enhanced—was another way in which the classical view of womanhood took center stage this season. It’s no surprise, then, that belts played such a pivotal role in the styling of collections: Some were extra-extra long, buckled once (and sometimes twice) around the body, then left to float limply down the legs, while others resembled a corset, wound tightly at the waist over coats or dresses. At Bally , Simone Bellotti left corsets half-open so they sat closer to the hips, taking a break from enforcing their strict beauty standards. title: Three New Collaborations Confirm it: 2026 Is the Year of the Y2K Sweatsuit Revival | Vogue
content: Three New Collaborations Confirm it: 2026 Is the Year of the Y2K Sweatsuit Revival | Vogue Skip to main content Your All Access Pass GET THE APP Search Account Menu Menu Menu FASHION RUNWAY SHOPPING BEAUTY CULTURE LIVING VOGUE BUSINESS TEEN VOGUE FASHION Expand Celebrity Style Street Style Models Designers Trends Sustainability All Fashion RUNWAY SHOPPING Chevron BEAUTY Expand Celebrity Beauty Hair Makeup Nails Wellness Skin All Beauty CULTURE Expand Opinion TV & Movies Music Arts Books News Sports All Culture LIVING Expand Homes Travel Food Parties Royals All Living WEDDINGS Expand Wedding Inspiration Planning & Advice For Wedding Guests The Bridal Shop All Weddings VOGUE BUSINESS Expand Technology Sustainability Fashion Retail Companies Beauty Events Member Benefits Executive Reports VIDEO PHOTOVOGUE MORE NEWSLETTERS PODCAST MAGAZINE PROJECTS VOGUE ARCHIVE TEEN VOGUE Search Search Contact Us About Vogue Trends Three New Collaborations Confirm It: 2026 Is the Year of the Y2K Sweatsuit Revival By Irene Kim , Taylor Lashley , and Laird Borrelli-Persson June 5, 2026 Sydney Lynn and Devon Lee Carlson wear the Madhappy x Freecity x Wildflower collaboration. Photo: Courtesy of Madhappy Save Story Save this story Save Story Save this story In the past two weeks, some of Gen Z’s favorite brands—like candy-colored Madhappy and cool-girl athleisure Sporty & Rich—have launched collaborations with millennial-loved labels like Freecity and Juicy Couture , known for their California-ready sweatsuits and tracksuits. The reception has been overwhelmingly positive, especially for the Madhappy x Freecity collab that led with Gen Z social media stars and sisters Devon Lee and Sydney Carlson. For Madhappy co-founder Noah Raf, the decision to collaborate with Freecity was easy, especially as a longtime LA resident. “I connected with Nina [of Freecity] a few years back, and it was one of those moments where you just hit it off with someone,” he said. “She is a true artist, and everything she does is entirely authentic. I grew up on Freecity, and in many ways, they have been an inspiration for what we’re doing at Madhappy: Raw. California. Optimism.” That mentality was echoed by Emily Oberg, creative director and founder of Sporty & Rich, who gushed about the significance of her collaboration with Juicy Couture. On Instagram , she posted “Juicy was THE brand of the Y2K generation and what Pam & Gela created was the first of its kind… Someone once said to me that Sporty & Rich was today’s version of Juicy, and I think that’s the best compliment I’ve ever got.” And then there’s designer Hiroaki Sueyasu of Kidill, who also has a Juicy Couture collaboration in the works. “I remember that from the late 1990s through the 2000s, Juicy Couture became hugely popular thanks to celebrities such as Paris Hilton and Britney Spears,” he noted. “In Japan, it gained widespread popularity through artists and celebrities, most notably Ayumi Hamasaki.” And as for the return of the iconi

title: Fashion Goals: Colm Dillane Reveals KidSuper’s Runway Move From Paris to Miami During the World Cup | Vogue
content: Fashion Goals: Colm Dillane Reveals KidSuper’s Runway Move From Paris to Miami During the World Cup | Vogue Skip to main content Your All Access Pass GET THE APP Search Account Menu Menu Menu FASHION RUNWAY SHOPPING BEAUTY CULTURE LIVING VOGUE BUSINESS TEEN VOGUE FASHION Expand Celebrity Style Street Style Models Designers Trends Sustainability All Fashion RUNWAY SHOPPING Chevron BEAUTY Expand Celebrity Beauty Hair Makeup Nails Wellness Skin All Beauty CULTURE Expand Opinion TV & Movies Music Arts Books News Sports All Culture LIVING Expand Homes Travel Food Parties Royals All Living WEDDINGS Expand Wedding Inspiration Planning & Advice For Wedding Guests The Bridal Shop All Weddings VOGUE BUSINESS Expand Technology Sustainability Fashion Retail Companies Beauty Events Member Benefits Executive Reports VIDEO PHOTOVOGUE MORE NEWSLETTERS PODCAST MAGAZINE PROJECTS VOGUE ARCHIVE TEEN VOGUE Search Search Contact Us About Vogue Runway Fashion Goals: Colm Dillane Reveals KidSuper’s Runway Move From Paris to Miami During the World Cup By Luke Leitch June 12, 2026 Photo: Courtesy of KidSuper Save Story Save this story Save Story Save this story “I am doing Paris Fashion Week,” insists Colm Dillane : “I’m just doing it from Miami.” KidSuper shows are always different, but its upcoming spring 2027 show will be especially so. That’s because this season its founder Dillane will present his new collection at the Nu Stadium in Miami during the World Cup. The show will kick off at the home ground of the David Beckham-founded Inter Miami CF on the evening (local time) of June 25. As ever, Dillane seems to be shooting for the stars: he says he’s planning for an audience of 2,500. But when you consider that the show falls between two particularly juicy World Cup fixtures in Miami—Scotland v Brazil on June 24, and Colombia v Portugal on June 27—it seems likely that there will be a healthy crowd of fans to come and spectate. “This is kind of my way to prove to everyone that I’m the real soccer guy [in fashion],” he says. “Especially when all these brands are now hopping on the train.” A photo of Dillane on his youth soccer team. Photo: Courtesy of KidSuper It’s true that Dillane has been playing soccer pretty much since he could kick a ball. “I grew up playing in New York City, and of the 25 kids that were on my team, 18 kids were from different countries,” he says. Before founding KidSuper he represented Brooklyn Technical High School and later NYU. One of the first things he decided on when developing the KidSuper HQ in Brooklyn was its rooftop soccer pitch, which hosts matches in the nascent KidSuper league. He cofounded another team, the Super Ninos, with J Balvin, and this season it was crowned Baller League USA champions. Past KidSuper soccer-related collabs have run from Pantofola d’Oro to Puma, while previous KidSuper shows have featured cameos from soccer legends including Brazil’s Ronaldinho and Italy’s Mario Balotelli. This World Cup, he says, KidSuper wil

title: How to Save 15% on Destree’s Fall Standouts | Vogue
content: How to Save 15% on Destree’s Fall Standouts | Vogue Skip to main content Your All Access Pass GET THE APP Search Account Menu Menu Menu FASHION RUNWAY SHOPPING BEAUTY CULTURE LIVING VOGUE BUSINESS TEEN VOGUE New Arrivals Summer in the City The Vacation Shop Under $300 Shoes Bags Editor Wish Lists FASHION Expand Celebrity Style Street Style Models Designers Trends Sustainability All Fashion RUNWAY SHOPPING Chevron BEAUTY Expand Celebrity Beauty Hair Makeup Nails Wellness Skin All Beauty CULTURE Expand Opinion TV & Movies Music Arts Books News Sports All Culture LIVING Expand Homes Travel Food Parties Royals All Living WEDDINGS Expand Wedding Inspiration Planning & Advice For Wedding Guests The Bridal Shop All Weddings VOGUE BUSINESS Expand Technology Sustainability Fashion Retail Companies Beauty Events Member Benefits Executive Reports VIDEO PHOTOVOGUE MORE NEWSLETTERS PODCAST MAGAZINE PROJECTS VOGUE ARCHIVE TEEN VOGUE Search Search Contact Us About Vogue Offers How to Save 15% on Destree’s Fall Standouts By Vogue October 1, 2025 Save Story Save this story Save Story Save this story Photo: Courtesy of Destree Destree’s devoted following reads like a dream dinner party guest list: Beyoncé, Rihanna, and Reese Witherspoon would all be rubbing puffed-up shoulders at the perfectly dressed table. Spoiler alert: big sleeves are a brand signature. Co-founded in 2016 by Géraldine Guyot-Arnault and Laetitia Lumbroso-Revenu, Destree is a ready-to-wear brand that reflects the style sensibilities of both Parisians and New Yorkers. Drawing inspiration from art, each collection is packed with exquisite fabrics, sculptural silhouettes, and painterly hues. For fall 2025 , however, this female-led label is plucking shades from a more muted color palette. The influencer-approved Amoako jacket is back. This time around it’s primed for cooler days (and nights) in a gray flannel that’s dotted with speckles and oozes sophistication. The best part? It taps into fall’s overtly feminine mood with delicate lace trims at the cuffs. Take the legwork out of styling by simply teaming yours with the matching Yoshimoto pants and a pair of polished derbies or fashion pumps . Elsewhere in the collection, there’s some crisp new shirting to take note of, too. Emblazoned with thick camel and white stripes, the Gahee poplin button-down has long, clean lines that hit flatteringly at the hip. It’s an office-ready match to the wide-leg Beatrice pants , or you could layer it with your favorite jeans and the Billie trench for brunch dates. Lastly, don’t sleep on this brand’s bags—after all, accessories were the start of Destree’s story. Reworked in brown suede for the new season , the ladylike Gunther bag is adorned with a textural swirl influenced by passementerie and French craftsmanship. Find our Vogue editor-approved picks below and shop them for less as an Insider. title: 11 Fall 2025 Shoe Trends to Know | Vogue
content: 11 Fall 2025 Shoe Trends to Know | Vogue Skip to main content Your All Access Pass GET THE APP Search Account Menu Menu Menu FASHION RUNWAY SHOPPING BEAUTY CULTURE LIVING VOGUE BUSINESS TEEN VOGUE New Arrivals Summer in the City The Vacation Shop Under $300 Shoes Bags Editor Wish Lists FASHION Expand Celebrity Style Street Style Models Designers Trends Sustainability All Fashion RUNWAY SHOPPING Chevron BEAUTY Expand Celebrity Beauty Hair Makeup Nails Wellness Skin All Beauty CULTURE Expand Opinion TV & Movies Music Arts Books News Sports All Culture LIVING Expand Homes Travel Food Parties Royals All Living WEDDINGS Expand Wedding Inspiration Planning & Advice For Wedding Guests The Bridal Shop All Weddings VOGUE BUSINESS Expand Technology Sustainability Fashion Retail Companies Beauty Events Member Benefits Executive Reports VIDEO PHOTOVOGUE MORE NEWSLETTERS PODCAST MAGAZINE PROJECTS VOGUE ARCHIVE TEEN VOGUE Search Search Contact Us About Vogue Fashion 11 Fall 2025 Shoe Trends to Shop Right Now By Alice Cary and Talia Abbas Edited by Madeline Fass and Libby Page October 30, 2025 Photos: Courtesy of Dries Van Noten, Stella McCartney, Calvin Klein, Prada, Saint Laurent, and Bally Save Story Save this story Save Story Save this story The line-up of fall 2025 shoe trends reads like a wishlist of wardrobe classics . A fresh batch of fashion sneakers had the front row craning their necks at the runway shows, including styles presented by Miuccia Prada, who has become a master purveyor of excellent off-duty footwear. Together with Raf Simons, Ms. Prada complemented paper bag-waistband skirts with leather sneakers, complete with intentionally-distressed details like scuffs and raw edges. At Miu Miu, meanwhile, the final product was quite the opposite: smooth leather trainers, realized in retro silhouettes , with intricately-stitched accents and buckles. In today’s comfort-driven age, these Miu Mius could fall into the “smart shoe” category, but for those with a penchant for more trad takes, an array of lace-ups also featured on the runways – Victoria Beckham, Rabanne and McQueen were but a few brands that demonstrated easy-to-achieve polish. Willing to embrace a heel? Knife-edged styles courtesy of Saint Laurent, gathered satin pumps at Dries Van Noten and pointed takes at Tom Ford will satiate a stiletto appetite. Here, Vogue highlights a hat-trick of standout fall 2025 shoe trends–and where to shop them, now–updated as retailer deliveries drop. While not new to fashion, we can only describe the material’s epic resurgence as exceptional. Brands are taking note and utilizing the luxe, soft handle for jackets , belts, kitten heels, and notably, handbags . The new crop of suede handbags on the market proves just how sophisticated, modern, and unique they can be. Vogue ’s Favorite Suede Bags: Featured in this article The Under-$300 Find J.Crew Berkeley bucket bag Jump to review $228 J.CREW The Editors’ Favorite Toteme belted suede tote Jump to review $1,300 NET-A-PORTER The Structured Bucket Tory Burch Romy suede hobo bag Jump to review $495 NORDSTROM The Slouchy Work Bag Zara maxi shopper bag Jump to review $219 ZARA The East-West Option Coach Empire 34 carryall bag Jump to review $450 COACH The Slouchy Shoulder Bag Quince slouchy shoulder bag Jump to review $150 QUINCE The Timeless Weekender Métier Vérité City braided suede tote Jump to review $4,450 NET-A-PORTER The Belted Hobo Bag Etro belted suede hobo bag Jump to review $2,150 NORDSTROM What’s additionally exciting is that, at the moment, designers are playing with a range of silhouettes that show off suede’s versatility. There are slouchy, relaxed shapes from The Row and Massimo Dutti—styles that pair easily with leather midiskirts and sporty outerwear. In that vein, Flattered and Gucci are reimagining the hobo bag, amplifying the bohemian nature of suede—in tones that range from chocolate brown and sandy beige. Likewise, polished shapes are trending, both in handbags and in outfit formulas, with structured top-handle shapes from Métier, Toteme, and J.Crew being the perfect counterpoint to ladylike looks. There are luxe investment styles that will stand the test of time, and more affordable options to experiment with the trend before plunging into the deep end. Ahead,

How to to Get the Ladylike Look for Fall By Laura Jackson September 17, 2024 Save Story Save this story Save Story Save this story Photo: Boe Marion All products featured on Vogue are independently selected by our editors. However, we may earn affiliate revenue on this article and commission when you buy something. With each new season, there’s typically one inescapable trend that not only captures our full attention, but our shopping carts, too. And this fall, that trend is ladylike dressing, beloved by both the runways—Toteme, Khaite, Prada!—and the street style scene . Ladylike dressing is all about the elevated wardrobe essential , but with a feminine twist–think polished midiskirts, seriously chic tailoring, kitten heel pumps , and elegant finishing touches such as a leather timepiece or a thin leather belt. And while the trend itself has a more sophisticated, uptown kind of vibe, it can be dressed down all the same. Vogue ’s Guide to Ladylike Dressing: The Collarless Blazer: Toteme wool-blend grain de poudre jacket , $1,040 The Modern Midi Dress: Reformation Renaie box-pleated dress , $248 The Kitten Heel Pump: Mango slingback heeled shoes with buckle , $80 The A-Line Skirt: Tory Burch faux croc skirt , $1,198 The Scarf Top: Róhe scarf-detailed sweater , $600 The Chic Carryall: Coach soft Empire carryall bag 40 , $695 The Preppy Polo: Le Kasha Fraga organic cashmere cardigan , $410 The Retro Sneaker: Dries Van Noten suede and leather sneakers , $475 The Peacoat: Cos double-faced wool peacoat , $275 The Elegant Belt: Anderson’s skinny oval buckle belt , $135 We first reported on this luxe new look last spring when polished ladylike jackets landed on the scene. And while the fitted jacket remains very much en-vogue, this season, it’s reimagined in a sleeker, more minimalist way. However, we may earn affiliate revenue on this article and commission when you buy something. If you were to ask Jennifer Lawrence, the Olsen twins , or any stylish Vogue editor in the office if they own a mesh flat or “naked shoe”—they’ll probably spout off a variety of styles they love and new ones they’re coveting. The mesh ballet flat really gained momentum in 2024, but it was The Row that initially pushed them into the spotlight in 2019. There’s no denying the shoe trend’s spotlight has been eclipsed in recent seasons, thanks to the growing popularity of flip-flops and beaded sandals , but Margot Robbie is here to remind us what makes this shoe endlessly appealing. Styled with clean tailoring and a minimalist sensibility, the barely-there, summer-ready shoe added just enough texture and intrigue without disrupting the look’s elegant foundation. The best part about them? Their airy pockets are perfect foil for warmer weather—discover our favorites, below. Crochet Flats An ode to craftsmanship, wear a crochet flat, like Zara’s or Alaïa’s, with other warm-weather essentials, like Bermuda shorts and poplin blouses. On the sprin g /summer 2026 runways , Max Mara, Balenciaga, and Khaite embraced the minimalist ’90s style, showing everything from sleek flats to playful wedges and many kitten heeled iterations. Editors and celebrities have followed suit, adopting the sandal for daily outfitting thanks to its effortless versatility. Vogue’s Thong Sandal Wish List: The Celeb-Approved Flat A.Emery Kinto flip flops Jump to review $180 NET-A-PORTER The Pop of Red Black Suede Studio Kendal 35 suede sandals Jump to review $300 NORDSTROM The Croc-Embossed Loeffler Randall Lina sandals Jump to review $250 NORDSTROM The Trending Wedge Jude Yaga wedge sandals Jump to review $580 NET-A-PORTER The Burst of Yellow Staud Freja thong kitten heel sandals Jump to review $295 SHOPBOP The Two-Tone Wedge Christopher Esber scala wedge sandals Jump to review $845 SHOPBOP The Minimalist Heel Khaite Archer sandals Jump to review $920 KHAITE I’ll admit, I was once skeptical. Something about exposing my toes on city sidewalks never felt quite right. But today’s best thong sandals—with playful pops of color, chic croc-embossed finishes, and minimalist silhouettes—quickly changed my mind. The beauty of the thong sandal lies in its ability to ground a look without competing with it. It channels an off-duty ease without reading unpolished or messy. Take note from Hailey Bieber and Kendall Jenner , who often wear theirs with worn-in denim and simple white tees for unfussy, California cool. title: Sorry Flip-Flops, All I Want to Wear Is a T-Strap Sandal | Vogue
content: Sorry Flip-Flops, All I Want to Wear Is a T-Strap Sandal | Vogue Skip to main content Your All Access Pass GET THE APP Search Account Menu Menu Menu FASHION RUNWAY SHOPPING BEAUTY CULTURE LIVING VOGUE BUSINESS TEEN VOGUE New Arrivals Summer in the City The Vacation Shop Under $300 Shoes Bags Editor Wish Lists FASHION Expand Celebrity Style Street Style Models Designers Trends Sustainability All Fashion RUNWAY SHOPPING Chevron BEAUTY Expand Celebrity Beauty Hair Makeup Nails Wellness Skin All Beauty CULTURE Expand Opinion TV & Movies Music Arts Books News Sports All Culture LIVING Expand Homes Travel Food Parties Royals All Living WEDDINGS Expand Wedding Inspiration Planning & Advice For Wedding Guests The Bridal Shop All Weddings VOGUE BUSINESS Expand Technology Sustainability Fashion Retail Companies Beauty Events Member Benefits Executive Reports VIDEO PHOTOVOGUE MORE NEWSLETTERS PODCAST MAGAZINE PROJECTS VOGUE ARCHIVE TEEN VOGUE Search Search Contact Us About Vogue Fashion Sorry Flip-Flops, All I Want to Wear This Summer Is a T-Strap Sandal By Andrea Zendejas June 10, 2026 Photographed by Michelle Huynh Save Story Save this story Save Story Save this story With all the fuss around the flip-flop and the wedge (not to mention the wedge flip-flop!), you might have missed the quiet comeback of an old summer favorite: the T-strap sandal. I’ve always had a soft spot for the barely-there style. A couple of years ago, I had a gold Prada pair that I wore into the ground. I promptly started borrowing my mother’s and sister’s, each a different color and finished with its own embellishment . Last year, I bought a coral pair that quickly became a go-to. Fast-forward to this season, and we’re seeing the T-strap reimagined with modern ease everywhere from Jil Sander and Toteme to Prada and Aquazzura. Some designs are rich in decorative details; others—like those at Jamie Haller, A.Emery, and Emme Parsons—lean into the simplicity of the sandal, stripping away adornment in favor of a clean, minimalist finish. Vogue ’s Favorite T-Strap Sandals: The Under-$300 Find ATP Atelier Alassia sandals Jump to review $290 NORDSTROM The Embellished Sandal Prada antiqued thong sandals Jump to review $1,200 PRADA The Everyday Pair A.Emery Alta sandals Jump to review $230 NET-A-PORTER The Pop of Color Emme Parsons Cecilia sandals Jump to review $565 SHOPBOP The Sporty Take Flòwze Flò sandals Jump to review $195 BANANA REPUBLIC The Heeled Must-Have Khaite Archer sandals Jump to review $890 KHAITE The enduring appeal of the T-strap sandal is that it can slip into any wardrobe with ease. Wear it with summer dresses , Bermuda shorts, or a slinky slip skirt—it’s a simple finishing touch that can be worn in more ways than one. The flip-flop may be everywhere, but the T-strap is right behind it. With its subtle sheen, the statement-making separate—in an array of colors and prints—has quickly proven to be summer’s most unexpected wardrobe staple , effortlessly styled with everything from fitted tank tops and flip-flops to crisp button-ups and heels. Vogue ’s Favorite Taffeta Pants The Editors’ Favorite Aflalo Marroc pants Jump to review $730 AFLALO The Cargo Pocket Donni. I picked up a sage green pair last June, while my colleague (and fellow Vogue Shopping editor) Andrea Zendejas acquired one of her own earlier this spring. content: How to Save 15% on Rùadh’s Small Batch Collections | Vogue Skip to main content Your All Access Pass GET THE APP Search Account Menu Menu Menu FASHION RUNWAY SHOPPING BEAUTY CULTURE LIVING VOGUE BUSINESS TEEN VOGUE New Arrivals Summer in the City The Vacation Shop Under $300 Shoes Bags Editor Wish Lists FASHION Expand Celebrity Style Street Style Models Designers Trends Sustainability All Fashion RUNWAY SHOPPING Chevron BEAUTY Expand Celebrity Beauty Hair Makeup Nails Wellness Skin All Beauty CULTURE Expand Opinion TV & Movies Music Arts Books News Sports All Culture LIVING Expand Homes Travel Food Parties Royals All Living WEDDINGS Expand Wedding Inspiration Planning & Advice For Wedding Guests The Bridal Shop All Weddings VOGUE BUSINESS Expand Technology Sustainability Fashion Retail Companies Beauty Events Member Benefits Executive Reports VIDEO PHOTOVOGUE MORE NEWSLETTERS PODCAST MAGAZINE PROJECTS VOGUE ARCHIVE TEEN VOGUE Search Search Contact Us About Vogue Offers How to Save 15% on Rùadh’s Small Batch Collections By Vogue October 1, 2025 Save Story Save this story Save Story Save this story Photo: Courtesy of Rùadh Put simply: Rùadh is the name on every cool-girl’s lips right now. Put more precisely, Rùadh (pronounced “roo-ah”) is the name on every cool-girl’s hips right now, given that jeans (among a few other well-executed essentials ) are the brand’s forte. Rùadh’s craft is fueled by founder Jac Cameron’s technical prowess when it comes to denim. Stacking up a knockout resumé since making the leap from Scotland to NYC in the early ’00s—including a stint as Calvin Klein’s global denim director—Cameron is well-versed in the art of combining her love for the quintessentially classic fabric with a passion for protecting the planet. It isn’t fluff to say that Rùadh’s small batch collections are purpose-driven and rooted in transparency. This certified B Corp makes it easy to discover everything there is to know about its clothes—from considered factory methods (like closed-loop water recycling systems) to thoughtful finishings. Billed as “an exploration in quiet resilience,” Rùadh’s fall 2025 drop includes a limited run of jeans that are clever wardrobe anchors. The Quinn jean is cut from a stiff Japanese denim that holds the straight-leg shape well but softens each time you pull them on. The charcoal wash will smarten up basics for desk-to-dinner wear, while the curved Rhone pair offers up a failsafe way to wear white denim this fall . Alongside jeans, Rùadh delivers everyday wardrobe essentials to complete modern capsules, like snuggly knits and cool outerwear. If a barn jacket is high on your new-season wish list , the Henderson hits all the right style notes and matches the cute Kenna mini skirt in tan. Both pieces are made using regenerative cotton and require little more styling than a great white tee and preppy loafers . Lastly, it would be remiss to not point out that Rùadh means “red” in Scottish Gaelic and is the label’s signature s

title: Small Batch and Stylish, Rùadh Is a New Brand to Watch | Vogue
content: Small Batch and Stylish, Rùadh Is a New Brand to Watch | Vogue Skip to main content Your All Access Pass GET THE APP Search Account Menu Menu Menu FASHION RUNWAY SHOPPING BEAUTY CULTURE LIVING VOGUE BUSINESS TEEN VOGUE FASHION Expand Celebrity Style Street Style Models Designers Trends Sustainability All Fashion RUNWAY SHOPPING Chevron BEAUTY Expand Celebrity Beauty Hair Makeup Nails Wellness Skin All Beauty CULTURE Expand Opinion TV & Movies Music Arts Books News Sports All Culture LIVING Expand Homes Travel Food Parties Royals All Living WEDDINGS Expand Wedding Inspiration Planning & Advice For Wedding Guests The Bridal Shop All Weddings VOGUE BUSINESS Expand Technology Sustainability Fashion Retail Companies Beauty Events Member Benefits Executive Reports VIDEO PHOTOVOGUE MORE NEWSLETTERS PODCAST MAGAZINE PROJECTS VOGUE ARCHIVE TEEN VOGUE Search Search Contact Us About Vogue Runway Small Batch and Stylish, Rùadh Is a New Brand to Watch By Laird Borrelli-Persson September 6, 2024 Save Story Save this story Save Story Save this story Rùadh Photo: Dan McMahon / Courtesy of Rùadh With her new brand Rùadh , Jac Cameron has chosen to take a “go small or go home approach” to fashion. An industry veteran and denim expert who co-founded AYR and was once Calvin Klein’s global denim director, Cameron knows from big. With this new project the idea is to really focus on craft and a connection to nature. (The company’s B Corp certification is pending.) “I just wanted to put something out there that really spoke to craftsmanship again; not a massive collection,” the designer said. There are “only 11 pieces for launch and every season I’ll be launching a new category that will be very tight—for next fall I’m exploring handknit sweaters in Scotland. I want to really make fashion feel like a cherished art form again.” Rùadh Photo: Dan McMahon / Courtesy of Rùadh Rùadh Photo: Dan McMahon / Courtesy of Rùadh Though based in New York for 20 years, Cameron grew up on a farm outside Glasgow. To photograph the first Rùadh collection, the designer made a nostalgic return to Caledonia—Elgin, to be exact—where models posed in golden fields  under a clear sky in denims that have a supple lightness and distinctive but gentle shapes. There’s much attention to detail here: the buttons on a pullover shirt were made in Italy from “about 80% recycled fiber,” she said; a seam above the ankle to create a curved leg line and jacket pockets feature a small vent. The pieces, many of regenerative cotton, are made in a smart factory in Los Angeles. “For me it’s about construction and making sure things are hand-touched,” Cameron said. Rùadh Photo: Dan McMahon / Courtesy of Rùadh Rùadh Photo: Dan McMahon / Courtesy of Rùadh For all the suppleness of the denim, the brand is built around strength. Rùadh (roo-ah) is a Scottish Gaelic word meaning red. It’s used to describe (stereotypically feisty) redheads, and, Cameron explained, “it is also associated with concepts of strength and quie

While mens’ barn jackets (a.k.a chore coats) originated in ’20s as utilitarian workwear, they’ve since been adopted for daily use and become a fixture in the wardrobes of everyone from the British royal family to Gigi Hadid. The jacket’s design details—the boxy silhouette, multiple pockets, button or snap closures, leather and cord collar, as well as its waxed, durable waterproof fabric—make it perfectly practical, but also give it its distinct no-fuss, unpretentious look. The simplicity and versatility of the jacket is akin to that of jeans. Fans of her eponymous label can expect a handful of hero styles each season, and for fall 2025, it’s this gorgeously draped blouson-sleeve midi which can carry you from desk-to-dusk with ease. Take style notes from our fall 2025 shoe trend report and contrast its femininity with a pair of polished derbies or modern brogues, before making the switch to kitten heels once the clock turns five. The nutty hue is a subtle transition from summer’s butter yellows, and will look effortlessly chic beneath a bold-shouldered coat—one of the 11 key finds we’re eyeing up for pre-fall. Relaxed tailoring is another everyday essential on our editor wish lists for the season ahead, and timeless yet modern tailoring is another string to Victoria Beckham’s bow. Pinstriped leather is the star of the show for pre-fall, but another much-loved house signature—the Alina trouser , a modern nod to ’70s-inspired hems—is back by popular demand. Tuck a scarf-detailed top or draped blouse into the high-rise waistband or wear yours as a sophisticated suit with the coordinating jackets in oregano and mauve . If you’re not ready to put your pins away just yet, there’s a pair of long tailored shorts in the British designer’s latest lineup that feel nicely transitional with crochet flats and light jackets. Last, but not least, score two new-season wins at once by adding something red (a top fall/winter 2025 color trend ) with a lace-trim to your basket. We can think of so many outfit formulas for this slinky rose-colored slip skirt —from boy tees to chunky knits—but the cool-girl way to wear chili pepper hues right now is tonally from head-to-toe. Don’t miss these burgundy slingback flats or the high-neck t

Not quite? The stifling heat may feel endless but signs of the new season are already showing: pre-fall collections are starting to trickle in. Before we make the full fall shift, there’s a brief transitional window—a liminal period that, similar to early days of summer, is the perfect time to get dressed. Summer favorites get paired with lightweight fall finds, and the resulting looks are layered, interesting, and deeply personal. There’s no single equation for how to approach pre-fall dressing, but the most compelling wardrobes embrace some kind of contrast. Is it a printed sarong over a trouser à la Dries Van Noten? A Chloé-esque lace cami underneath a new leather jacket silhouette? Maybe it’s a pencil skirt with oversized shirting and a flip-flop . Some pre-fall styles are carryovers from the past seasons; others offer entirely novel takes (in shape or styling) on familiar staples.
