However, we may earn affiliate revenue on this article and commission when you buy something. As spring’s much-anticipated arrival inches closer, there’s no time like the present to start transitioning your closet for the in-between weather—those weeks when the cold one day feels like February and the next there’s a warm breeze in the air that hints at May. Fortunately, new-season collections have just landed, and the virtual aisles are already stacked with the It items you’ll soon be seeing everywhere. Think lightweight outerwear, comfy knits, crisp button-ups , all-in-one dresses , and more. And when in doubt, throwing in a personality pick (leopard print, a pop of bold color, or a festive jewel) will certainly cure any mid-season wardrobe blues—plus take your look to new levels entirely. Whether you approach it with a bohemian mindset like Chemena Kamali’s Chloé with a feathery cami or blouse; a little more sultry like Anthony Vaccarello’s Saint Laurent with a boudoir-inspired midi, or a bit cooler like The Row with a lace-trimmed tunic irreverently styled over pants, is entirely up to you. Even better, brands like Doên, Reformation, and J.Crew are creating pieces that feel just as special as their runway counterparts. From fancy dinners to work celebrations to, yes, even weddings , the appeal of these party dresses lies in their versatility: With the right accessories, that sequin mini dress you’ve been eyeing works just as well for your friend’s courthouse celebration as it does an upcoming birthday. Why not add a few extra pieces to the rotation? Think of it as manifesting more festivity, one lace-trimmed slip dress at a time. Typically, a “ cocktail attire ” dress code means something knee-length or a midi dress (though shorter styles done in a luxe fabric, like taffeta or silk organza, could also be appropriate depending on the venue). Steer clear of floor-length gowns in favor of breezier silhouettes, like an ankle-grazing halterneck or something with an asymmetrical handkerchief hemline. If you’re shopping more generally, anything goes. A teal midi finished with grey fringe? Absolutely. An electric blue sequined dress—why not? A clingy cowl neck number that looks straight out of a ’90s fever dream (in the best way)? Sure. content: Shop Another Tomorrow’s Modern Essentials at 20% Off | Vogue Skip to main content Your All Access Pass GET THE APP Search Account Menu Menu Menu FASHION RUNWAY SHOPPING BEAUTY CULTURE LIVING VOGUE BUSINESS TEEN VOGUE New Arrivals Summer in the City The Vacation Shop Under $300 Shoes Bags Editor Wish Lists FASHION Expand Celebrity Style Street Style Models Designers Trends Sustainability All Fashion RUNWAY SHOPPING Chevron BEAUTY Expand Celebrity Beauty Hair Makeup Nails Wellness Skin All Beauty CULTURE Expand Opinion TV & Movies Music Arts Books News Sports All Culture LIVING Expand Homes Travel Food Parties Royals All Living WEDDINGS Expand Wedding Inspiration Planning & Advice For Wedding Guests The Bridal Shop All Weddings VOGUE BUSINESS Expand Technology Sustainability Fashion Retail Companies Beauty Events Member Benefits Executive Reports VIDEO PHOTOVOGUE MORE NEWSLETTERS PODCAST MAGAZINE PROJECTS VOGUE ARCHIVE TEEN VOGUE Search Search Contact Us About Vogue Offers Shop Another Tomorrow’s Modern Essentials at 20% Off By Vogue July 21, 2025 Save Story Save this story Save Story Save this story Photo: Courtesy of Another Tomorrow If it feels like you never have anything to wear, you don’t have to look too far to find mindful additions that’ll make your wardrobe work that much harder. For Vogue editors, it’s Another Tomorrow. The New York-based label focuses on consciously crafted staples rooted in a sustainable design ethos. Creative director Elizabeth Giardina joined Another Tomorrow in 2020, emphasizing raw material choices that reflect her own edited wardrobe of not just owning fewer but better items, but rather, the best versions of those pieces—and then holding onto them for as long as she can. At Another Tomorrow, you’ll find everything from a silk cocoon blouse that can be cinched at the hem or worn loose as a tunic to elegant dresses that can be dressed up or down to organic denim that only gets better with wear. Like Giardina previously told Vogue , she is “designing for the life that I’m trying to lead, which is a life that’s fairly effortless, that leaves time for other things.” Shop a few editor-favorites from the latest collection for less as an Insider, below. title: 9-5: How Another Tomorrow’s Elizabeth Giardina Achieves the Ultimate Effortless Work Uniform | Vogue
content: 9-5: How Another Tomorrow’s Elizabeth Giardina Achieves the Ultimate Effortless Work Uniform | Vogue Skip to main content Your All Access Pass GET THE APP Search Account Menu Menu Menu FASHION RUNWAY SHOPPING BEAUTY CULTURE LIVING VOGUE BUSINESS TEEN VOGUE New Arrivals Summer in the City The Vacation Shop Under $300 Shoes Bags Editor Wish Lists FASHION Expand Celebrity Style Street Style Models Designers Trends Sustainability All Fashion RUNWAY SHOPPING Chevron BEAUTY Expand Celebrity Beauty Hair Makeup Nails Wellness Skin All Beauty CULTURE Expand Opinion TV & Movies Music Arts Books News Sports All Culture LIVING Expand Homes Travel Food Parties Royals All Living WEDDINGS Expand Wedding Inspiration Planning & Advice For Wedding Guests The Bridal Shop All Weddings VOGUE BUSINESS Expand Technology Sustainability Fashion Retail Companies Beauty Events Member Benefits Executive Reports VIDEO PHOTOVOGUE MORE NEWSLETTERS PODCAST MAGAZINE PROJECTS VOGUE ARCHIVE TEEN VOGUE Search Search Contact Us About Vogue Fashion 9-5: How Another Tomorrow’s Elizabeth Giardina Achieves the Ultimate Effortless Work Uniform By Talia Abbas June 28, 2024 Photo: Courtesy of Stefano Ortega Save Story Save this story Save Story Save this story Elizabeth Giardina aspires to have the most edited wardrobe possible. To her, that’s less about owning “fewer but better” items, but rather, owning the best versions of those pieces—and then holding onto them for as long as she can. It’s a mindset that not only aligns with her personal values, but with her work as creative director of Another Tomorrow as well. Giardina joined the New York-based label nearly three years ago after being tapped by founder Vanessa Barboni Hallik , who founded the company in 2020 in the hopes of creating elegant, timeless clothing that was also ethically and sustainably made. Giardina, who grew up in Missouri and studied fine art as an undergraduate at Washington University in St. Louis, tells Vogue she always wanted to work as a designer. She moved to New York in 2005, where she helmed creative teams at Derek Lam and Proenza Schouler, and helped build out the 10 Crosby and White Label divisions for both brands. Of her current role, she says that the design approach she takes is unlike anything she’s done in the past. “Instead of starting from a concept, I have to really think about what I can ethically get and what materials are available to me,” she says. “The idea of thinking about design that starts from raw materials is more holistic and rich, and something that I never had the privilege of doing before.” Giardina says working this closely with suppliers and mills has helped her form a connection to the earth, which in turn has made her “a more patient and thoughtful designer.” Adds Giardina, “If a material doesn’t work out, I can’t just source anything in the market for production. The way that we’re working has really forced me to slow down and to problem solve in ways that I’ve never had to.” This

title: Wardrobe.NYC Brings Its Minimalist Touch to H&M in New Capsule Collection | Vogue
content: Wardrobe.NYC Brings Its Minimalist Touch to H&M in New Capsule Collection | Vogue Skip to main content Your All Access Pass GET THE APP Search Account Menu Menu Menu FASHION RUNWAY SHOPPING BEAUTY CULTURE LIVING VOGUE BUSINESS TEEN VOGUE FASHION Expand Celebrity Style Street Style Models Designers Trends Sustainability All Fashion RUNWAY SHOPPING Chevron BEAUTY Expand Celebrity Beauty Hair Makeup Nails Wellness Skin All Beauty CULTURE Expand Opinion TV & Movies Music Arts Books News Sports All Culture LIVING Expand Homes Travel Food Parties Royals All Living WEDDINGS Expand Wedding Inspiration Planning & Advice For Wedding Guests The Bridal Shop All Weddings VOGUE BUSINESS Expand Technology Sustainability Fashion Retail Companies Beauty Events Member Benefits Executive Reports VIDEO PHOTOVOGUE MORE NEWSLETTERS PODCAST MAGAZINE PROJECTS VOGUE ARCHIVE TEEN VOGUE Search Search Contact Us About Vogue Runway Wardrobe.NYC Brings Its Minimalist Touch to H&M in a New Capsule Collection By Alexandra Hildreth June 23, 2026 Wardrobe.NYC founders Josh Goot and Christine Centenera Photo: H&M Save Story Save this story Save Story Save this story Stella McCartney. Glenn Martens. Wardrobe.NYC? The near-decade-old label, founded by Josh Goot and Vogue Australia editor in chief Christine Centenera, may not be as globally renowned as H&M’s recent designer partners, but the mass retailer is passionate about the minimalist-driven collaboration. When H&M first approached Wardrobe.NYC a little more than a year ago, there was immediate buy-in. According to Goot, the goal was straightforward and not too dissimilar from Wardrobe NYC’s own: to build the perfect capsule collection. “You don’t need any more pieces than you will have in this collection, to be honest,” Ann-Sofie Johansson, H&M’s creative adviser and head of design, tells Vogue . “You can just have them on constant rotation and create new looks.” Photo: H&M The lineup, which launches online and in select H&M stores on August 6, draws from Wardrobe.NYC’s sleek archive. “We looked back at our work and distilled down what we thought was the most essential, the most timeless, yet still of the moment that could combine to create a singular statement,” says Goot. At first glance, a T-shirt bodysuit may feel simple, but like any good elevated basic, it has a special twist (in this case, statement–making padded shoulders). The tailored denim miniskirt has something special going on too—its exposed seams give it an edgy corset-like feel. For shoes, there’s no need to choose between heeled flip-flops and patent go-go boots: The two silhouettes are mashed into one. Photo: H&M Before she was EIC at Vogue Australia, Centenera was a stylist whose credits include working with Virgil Abloh at Louis Vuitton in Paris. As someone who is constantly surrounded by clothes, she sees the value in keeping a simple but effective wardrobe. As a result, each piece, from a boxy blazer to a slim, tailored midiskirt, possesses an additive qu

title: Katie Holmes Delivers Two New Takes on One Hot Summer Trend | Vogue
content: Katie Holmes Delivers Two New Takes on One Hot Summer Trend | Vogue Skip to main content Your All Access Pass GET THE APP Search Account Menu Menu Menu FASHION RUNWAY SHOPPING BEAUTY CULTURE LIVING VOGUE BUSINESS TEEN VOGUE FASHION Expand Celebrity Style Street Style Models Designers Trends Sustainability All Fashion RUNWAY SHOPPING Chevron BEAUTY Expand Celebrity Beauty Hair Makeup Nails Wellness Skin All Beauty CULTURE Expand Opinion TV & Movies Music Arts Books News Sports All Culture LIVING Expand Homes Travel Food Parties Royals All Living WEDDINGS Expand Wedding Inspiration Planning & Advice For Wedding Guests The Bridal Shop All Weddings VOGUE BUSINESS Expand Technology Sustainability Fashion Retail Companies Beauty Events Member Benefits Executive Reports VIDEO PHOTOVOGUE MORE NEWSLETTERS PODCAST MAGAZINE PROJECTS VOGUE ARCHIVE TEEN VOGUE Search Search Contact Us About Vogue Celebrity Style Katie Holmes Delivers Two New Takes on One Hot Summer Trend By Christian Allaire June 8, 2026 Photo: Getty Images Save Story Save this story Save Story Save this story It’s our favorite time of year: Katie Hopmes season. Every summer, as summer settles in and temperatures start warming up in New York, Katie Holmes materializes on the streets of Manhattan—almost as though on cue. It’s like Holmes knows that we’re in desperate need of summery style inspo. After all, with one simple paparazzi sighting, the star has the ability to set trends and drive fashion forward. Remember that summer she ignited a whole knit Khaite bra craze ? Or embraced the (polarizing) square-toe trend a few years back, and suddenly everyone started wearing square toes ? That’s the Katie Holmes effect. So what does our fashion prognosticator have on tap for this summer? Holmes was spotted out and about in the city this weekend wearing two fresh takes on an enduring summer trend: Boho-chic dressing. Photo: Getty Images Yes, we’re talking about the groovy, free-spirited pieces that once dominated the 2000s —including maxi dresses, lots of lace, and maybe a chunky belt thrown in for good measure. Such boho staples have been making a comeback as of late, thanks to the recent collections at Chloé and Isabel Marant, amongst others. But for summer 2026, Holmes is taking it in a decidedly more edgy direction. The star first wore a silky, champagne-colored Chloé dress to a Tribeca Film Festival screening this weekend, complete with lace trimming and puffed shoulders (from the label’s fall 2026 collection). She paired it with the label’s black pointed Cleia sandals with chunky gold studs—a tougher shoe to counteract the breeziness of her frock. Later in the night, for the after-party, Holmes then followed it up with a peachy Magda Butrym silk-satin lace slip dress, worn over black leather pants (again, to toughen things up). Her two distinct approaches to boho-chic style are clearly about making the look less hyperfeminine or soft, and much more grungy. The question is. . . what do we call t

title: Are the Spring 2027 Menswear Shows Closing the Swag Gap? | Vogue
content: Are the Spring 2027 Menswear Shows Closing the Swag Gap? | Vogue Skip to main content Your All Access Pass GET THE APP Search Account Menu Menu Menu FASHION RUNWAY SHOPPING BEAUTY CULTURE LIVING VOGUE BUSINESS TEEN VOGUE FASHION Expand Celebrity Style Street Style Models Designers Trends Sustainability All Fashion RUNWAY SHOPPING Chevron BEAUTY Expand Celebrity Beauty Hair Makeup Nails Wellness Skin All Beauty CULTURE Expand Opinion TV & Movies Music Arts Books News Sports All Culture LIVING Expand Homes Travel Food Parties Royals All Living WEDDINGS Expand Wedding Inspiration Planning & Advice For Wedding Guests The Bridal Shop All Weddings VOGUE BUSINESS Expand Technology Sustainability Fashion Retail Companies Beauty Events Member Benefits Executive Reports VIDEO PHOTOVOGUE MORE NEWSLETTERS PODCAST MAGAZINE PROJECTS VOGUE ARCHIVE TEEN VOGUE Search Search Contact Us About Vogue Runway Are the Spring 2027 Menswear Shows Closing the Swag Gap? By Alexandra Hildreth June 26, 2026 Dries Van Noten, Auralee, and Ralph Lauren, spring 2027 menswear Collage by Vogue Save Story Save this story Save Story Save this story Are you shrekking in your relationship? Are Jacob Elordi and Kendall Jenner the perfect swag match? By the way, are people still talking about swag gaps? Forget people, can the runways have them too? Confused? Here are some cliff notes: A “swag gap” is a viral term popularized last year when people began lamenting online about their relationship’s vast sartorial differences. As for Jacob Elordi and Kendall Jenner, two attractive lanky people who possess a penchant for quiet luxury and were recently spotted on a walk in Australia in matching gorpcore, yeah, it seems like a pretty good fit. And menswear? Well, it’s long been the also-ran in fashion, but observing the particularly funky men’s shows this season, I’d say that men’s fashion week might just be closing the swag gap with its flashier womenswear counterpart. Ralph Lauren, spring 2027 menswear Photo: Isidore Montag / Gorunway.com There are a few key factors that have jazzed up this season’s collections. Reporting live from on the ground in Paris, Vogue Runway senior fashion news editor Max Berlinger chalks it up partly to accessories. “I’ve noticed a ton of small, quirky gestures like the anklets at Auralee , brooches at Givenchy and Dior , and lots of scarves,” he says. Sarongs, which first appeared in Julian Klausner’s Dries Van Noten menswear are here to stay: sarongs and shirts wrapped around the waist channeled a breezy California attitude at Julian Louie’s Aubero . Elsewhere, Ralph Lauren’s Polo portion dialed up the styling several notches, piling on RL camo corduroy hats with sweaters around the waist, with pocket squares with duffel bags, while bracelet stacks complemented—and never clashed—with instarsia knits. It’s refreshing to see such unabashed maximalism on men, and the best part is that it inspires at-home experimentation. There’s no excuse to be in a swag deficit, wh

Mentally already there? If there’s one place to shop for vacation essentials , it’s Janessa Leone. The Los Angeles-based label known for its high-quality accessories has a vast array of straw hats and bags for your next sun-soaked getaway. Headed to the South of France? Or maybe the Balearic Islands? May we suggest the Felix: it’s a packable bucket hat with a classic lantern shape that feels very Audrey Hepburn in Two for the Road when you flip the brim. Ibiza-bound? Show up to Casa Jondal in the Teagan, a floppier, boho-inspired style with an adjustable brim. Seeking sun with a pocket of shade in St. Barths? The Tinsley’s wide brim and tall crown matches the island’s private glamour. Transport any one of these in the Tanner, a roomy raffia tote that can double as a beach bag and a hat carrier; it’s trimmed with regenerative leather straps should you not want to fold your hat in half. A lightweight handwoven beach blanket , petite raffia bucket bag , and crochet cover-up round out the summer assortment. Shop these editor favorites for 15% off as an Insider, then put your out-of-office dates on the calendar. Bon voyage! Sunscreen is half the battle; a packable sun hat both keeps you safe from harmful rays and adds the finishing touch to your resortwear . The catch? This is, famously, one of the trickier accessories to puzzle into your luggage. Our answer comes in the form of over 30 options that are flexible, foldable, and rollable; they’re designed to tuck neatly into your luggage , and still look brand new once you reach your beachy destination . The Best Packable Sun Hats, According to Vogue The Beach Cloche Toteme raffia-like sunhat Jump to review $290 NET-A-PORTER The Baseball Cap Ralph Lauren The Iconic Chino ball cap Jump to review $55 RALPH LAUREN The Raffia Bucket Hat Free People Aneta rafia bucket hat Jump to review $68 FREE PEOPLE The Cowboy Hat Janessa Leoné Sacha straw cowboy hat Jump to review $287 NORDSTROM The Visor Lack of Color linen visor Jump to review $109 REVOLVE The Crochet One Loewe x Paula's Ibiza Anagram bucket hat Jump to review $790 NET-A-PORTER The Sailor Hat Zara raffia skimmer hat Jump to review $46 ZARA The Printed Canvas Miu Miu poplin bucket hat Jump to review $595 MIU MIU Channel tropical vibes with your chapeau, via a brightly patterned bucket hat or a striped crochet option. If your vacation wardrobe follows the same minimalist bent as your workwear , stay true to style with something in natural straw or black raffia. Feeling more daring? A soft cowboy hat that folds up nicely or a compact sailor hat is a fun way to spice up your out-of-office style. Clothing must be easy and, more important, breezy. Luckily, several of the season’s most-wanted pieces fit the bill, from lightweight poplin dresses and bubble-shaped balloon pants to the forever-timeless menswear-inspired button-up shirt. When it comes to great shirting, however, few styles are as versatile—or romantic—as the billowy white blouse. Vogue ’s Summer Guide to Billowy White Tops The Dainty Broderie Anglaise Sea Eliza blouse Jump to review $265 NET-A-PORTER The Embroidered Cap Sleeve J.Crew embroidered cap-sleeve top Jump to review $98 J.CREW The Anything Dôen Dôen Frederica blouse Jump to review $170 NET-A-PORTER The Fitted Tank Staud Matilde top Jump to review $245 NET-A-PORTER The Sweet Boatneck Posse Marilyn blouse Jump to review $270 POSSE The Tie-Detailed Top Skall Studio Freesia pleated shirt Jump to review $250 NET-A-PORTER The Sleek Scoop-Neck Banana Republic Breezy scoop-neck top Jump to review $90 BANANA REPUBLIC The Celeb Favorite Alex Mill Collette top Jump to review $215 NORDSTROM Kaia Gerber leaving the Library Science book reading at Hollywood Books in Los Angeles in May. Photo: Backgrid As with any closet classic, you’d need little convincing as to why the billowy white top works. Still, if you’re looking for a little inspiration, consider this your sign from Kaia Gerber . Earlier this spring, she stepped out in a chic cropped version with simple blue jeans and a pair of black ballet flats . The look perfectly summed up how we want to dress this summer: modern boho and entirely cool. While Gerber styled hers for a night out, the warm-weather essential offers endless styling possibilities and works for just about any occasion. Dressing for a balmy morning commute? The Wedge Sandal—Here’s How to Wear It By Laura Jackson June 11, 2026 Photographed by Acielle / StyleDuMonde Save Story Save this story Save Story Save this story If this summer’s shoe trends have anything in common, it’s that all our favorite styles were born in the ’ 90s . There’s the sleek strappy sandal beloved by the likes of Carolyn Bessette-Kennedy and Kate Moss, as well as the flip-flop kitten heel—a runway mainstay at iconic labels of the era, including Calvin Klein, Helmut Lang, and Michael Kors—and sported by today’s It girls, including Hailey Bieber, Kendall Jenner, Zoë Kravitz, and more. The nostalgia doesn’t stop there. Summer wedges —a hallmark of late-’90s and early-aughts dressing—have returned with fresh appeal. Cate Holstein, for example, made the wedge a centerpiece of Khaite’s spring 2026 collection, pairing the sculptural silhouette with everything from printed dresses to tapered trousers . Elsewhere, contemporary labels like Le Monde Béryl, St. Agni, Jude, and Neous have put their own minimalist spin on the shape, proving the once-divisive style is more than ready for a comeback. It was an era of pillbox hats and glamorous coats. (It was also a time of cigarette smoke-filled cabins, so we’ll take our wins where we can.) But when it comes to today’s travel outfits , suffice it to say the reality of dressing for a flight, no matter how short or long, is typically uninspiring. It’s time to change that: While you’re planning your vacation wardrobe , picking out something to wear to feel put together on the trip itself can make long travel days a little more special. (And, with the right knitwear , a lot more cozy.) content: The Best Gucci Handbags (and Their Histories) to Shop Right Now, From the Jackie to the Borsetto | Vogue Skip to main content Your All Access Pass GET THE APP Search Account Menu Menu Menu FASHION RUNWAY SHOPPING BEAUTY CULTURE LIVING VOGUE BUSINESS TEEN VOGUE New Arrivals Summer in the City The Vacation Shop Under $300 Shoes Bags Editor Wish Lists FASHION Expand Celebrity Style Street Style Models Designers Trends Sustainability All Fashion RUNWAY SHOPPING Chevron BEAUTY Expand Celebrity Beauty Hair Makeup Nails Wellness Skin All Beauty CULTURE Expand Opinion TV & Movies Music Arts Books News Sports All Culture LIVING Expand Homes Travel Food Parties Royals All Living WEDDINGS Expand Wedding Inspiration Planning & Advice For Wedding Guests The Bridal Shop All Weddings VOGUE BUSINESS Expand Technology Sustainability Fashion Retail Companies Beauty Events Member Benefits Executive Reports VIDEO PHOTOVOGUE MORE NEWSLETTERS PODCAST MAGAZINE PROJECTS VOGUE ARCHIVE TEEN VOGUE Search Search Contact Us About Vogue INSIDE THE IT BAG Gucci Handbags 101: A Guide to Shopping Its Iconic Bags, From the Jackie to the Borsetto By Lilah Ramzi June 12, 2026 Photo: Courtesy of Backgrid, Daniel Zuchnik, XNY/Star Max, Ricky Vigil, Getty Images Save Story Save this story Save Story Save this story All products featured on Vogue are independently selected by our editors. However, we may earn affiliate revenue on this article and commission when you buy something. A History of the Best Gucci Handbags In 1897, a man named Guccio Gucci left his native Florence for London, where he worked at the tony Savoy Hotel as a bellhop. Handling the luggage of the ritzy clientele there gave him the education he needed to return to Italy and produce his very own line of travel-centric leather goods. The year was 1921 when Guccio finally opened the doors to his boutique on Florence’s Via della Vigna Nuova, selling imported suitcases in addition to goods handcrafted by local artisans. It didn’t take long for customers to latch onto Guccio’s wares, and soon after that, Gucci became an outright sensation. The Best Gucci Handbags to Shop Now The Jackie 1961 Gucci Jackie Slim medium bag Jump to review $2,450 GUCCI The Gucci Horsebit 1955 Gucci Horsebit 1955 small shoulder bag Jump to review $3,290 GUCCI The Borsetto Gucci Borsetto large Boston bag Jump to review $3,450 GUCCI The Madison Gucci Madison medium shoulder bag Jump to review $1,900 GUCCI The Mercato Gucci Mercato small tote bag Jump to review $2,350 GUCCI The Giglio Gucci Giglio bag Jump to review $2,250 GUCCI The Woven Tote Gucci Woven small tote bag Jump to review $290 GUCCI The Ophidia Gucci Ophidia small Boston bag Jump to review $2,100 GUCCI A trade embargo placed on Italy during Mussolini’s rule meant that materials—leather in particular—were scarce. The Australian label, founded by sisters Ilona Hammer and Peta Heinsen in 2015, focuses on classic summer styles that bridge simplicity with modernity—an ethos that resonates strongly with many Vogue editors who seek them out for seasonal yet timeless wardrobe additions. Matteau’s consciously-designed swimwear is a great place to start if you’re just getting acquainted with the brand, but since launching over a decade ago, the label’s expanded into various ready-to-wear categories that prioritize regenerative, organic, and recycled materials like poplin, silk, and regenerated nylon. Whether it’s for summer-in-the city dressing or laidback vacation wear, mainstays in our own closets include their fisherman drawstring pant , which associate fashion director Madeline Fass recently wore on a trip to Galicia. Associate shopping director Talia Abbas loves their white cotton poplin midi, which she’s had for two summers and counting. Meanwhile, Cortne Bonilla swears by their airy popover dress for beating the heat in style, even more so now that she’s expecting. Shop these editor favorites for less as an Insider—and discover more Vogue -loved styles that won’t compete with your wardrobe, but instead, complete it. It should pull its weight, appeal to any personality, and fit in seamlessly with various aesthetics. To put this theory to the test, we’ve asked three Vogue editors to style the same piece, each in their own way. Slung on a leather cord or chain, the pendant necklace is more than just a necklace, it’s a personal touch—and for spring/summer 2026 , brands confirmed the accessory as a summer must-have. Dries Van Noten sent shell pendants down the runway, while TWP presented more functional objects. On the market, you can find everything from leather key rings to sculptural shapes crafted from organic materials. Vogue editors are loyal to the ones they wear: Justin Fernandez, contributing menswear editor, has a Celine letter pendant he reaches for like clockwork once summer starts. Concetta Ciarlo, our beauty shopping writer, meanwhile, has formed a deep connection to Vertigo tiger’s eye pendant. As for myself? I’ve been building a small collection, which all started with Hunting Season’s tagua pendant. Ahead, a look at how each of us styles it. Justin Fernandez, contributing menswear editor “One of my most cherished piece is my Celine Phoebe Philo era ‘J’ pendant necklace. I have a habit of reaching for it as soon as summer comes around every year. I love to wear it relaxingly low—and it goes perfectly as a little pop against an all white outfit. A very coveted item that was snatched off my neck after a night in Berlin… true story, but for another time.” eBay Editor’s Pick Save to wishlist Save to wishlist Celine alphabet charm necklace $1,330 EBAY Save to wishlist Save to wishlist Polo Ralph Lauren button-down linen shirt $170 MR PORTER Save to wishlist Save to wishlist Canali reversible belt $350 MR PORTER Save to wishlist Save to wishlist Agolde Magnus wide-leg jeans $230 MR PORT

title: The Unexpected Joy of Shopping Your Local Hardware Store | Vogue
content: The Unexpected Joy of Shopping Your Local Hardware Store | Vogue Skip to main content Your All Access Pass GET THE APP Search Account Menu Menu Menu FASHION RUNWAY SHOPPING BEAUTY CULTURE LIVING VOGUE BUSINESS TEEN VOGUE FASHION Expand Celebrity Style Street Style Models Designers Trends Sustainability All Fashion RUNWAY SHOPPING Chevron BEAUTY Expand Celebrity Beauty Hair Makeup Nails Wellness Skin All Beauty CULTURE Expand Opinion TV & Movies Music Arts Books News Sports All Culture LIVING Expand Homes Travel Food Parties Royals All Living WEDDINGS Expand Wedding Inspiration Planning & Advice For Wedding Guests The Bridal Shop All Weddings VOGUE BUSINESS Expand Technology Sustainability Fashion Retail Companies Beauty Events Member Benefits Executive Reports VIDEO PHOTOVOGUE MORE NEWSLETTERS PODCAST MAGAZINE PROJECTS VOGUE ARCHIVE TEEN VOGUE Search Search Contact Us About Vogue Homes The Unexpected Joy of Shopping Your Local Hardware Store By Angela Tafoya June 17, 2026 Photographed by Patrick Demarchelier, Vogue, July 2004 Save Story Save this story Save Story Save this story These days, when you need a commonplace household item, a quick click of a button will zoom it on over, sometimes even the same day. But not so long ago, when you wanted to browse a catchall shop for everything from paint colors to wood glue to paper towel holders to waste bins, you got up, left your house, and went to your local hardware shop. Nothing fancy or over the top—just a brick-and-mortar filled with thousands of small items to keep a home running. Now, there’s an unexpected joy that happens when you go and actually browse the aisles. Maybe it’s the thrill of discovering quirky bric-a-brac for your dresser, or finding items that ignite a DIY decor idea—but designers agree that these days there’s a specific spark you get when wandering your local hardware store that can’t be replicated online. “The smell alone—a faint mix of sawdust, metal, fertilizer, and possibility—feels deeply nostalgic and oddly comforting,” says Christine Gachot of Gachot Studios . “I always leave with things I didn't know I needed but suddenly cannot live without.” Part of what makes the hardware store so creatively adventurous, designers say, is what it isn’t: a showroom. There’s no narrative or overly styled vignettes. “A hardware store presents inventory. It’s relentless, indifferent, and democratic,” says Brittney Hart, co-founder of the design studio Husband Wife . “The absence of agenda invites projection.” As Jordan Mosslar, founder of LA-based Form LA , puts it: “Nothing is trying to persuade you. It allows you to see materials for what they are and imagine new possibilities.” And for a lot of hardware stores, they go beyond the nut bolts (literally), offering a distinct selection of items that designers genuinely love and put to clever use. Jessica Alpert of Jessica Alpert Design shares that for her, Liz’s Antique Hardware in Los Angeles delivers on that note. It’s less a hardware

content: How to Buy an Antique Engagement Ring, Like Devon Lee Carlson and Taylor Swift | Vogue Skip to main content Your All Access Pass GET THE APP Search Account Menu Menu Menu FASHION RUNWAY SHOPPING BEAUTY CULTURE LIVING VOGUE BUSINESS TEEN VOGUE Wedding Inspiration Planning & Advice For Wedding Guests The Bridal Shop FASHION Expand Celebrity Style Street Style Models Designers Trends Sustainability All Fashion RUNWAY SHOPPING Chevron BEAUTY Expand Celebrity Beauty Hair Makeup Nails Wellness Skin All Beauty CULTURE Expand Opinion TV & Movies Music Arts Books News Sports All Culture LIVING Expand Homes Travel Food Parties Royals All Living WEDDINGS Expand Wedding Inspiration Planning & Advice For Wedding Guests The Bridal Shop All Weddings VOGUE BUSINESS Expand Technology Sustainability Fashion Retail Companies Beauty Events Member Benefits Executive Reports VIDEO PHOTOVOGUE MORE NEWSLETTERS PODCAST MAGAZINE PROJECTS VOGUE ARCHIVE TEEN VOGUE Search Search Contact Us About Vogue Weddings How to Buy an Antique Engagement Ring, Like Devon Lee Carlson and Taylor Swift By Anna Cafolla and Laura Hawkins June 21, 2026 Photo: Instagram (@devonleecarlson) Save Story Save this story Save Story Save this story “When Taylor Swift came out with her beautiful old mine-cut diamond, I think every jewelry dealer in the world was delighted,” says antique jewelry specialist Catherine Taylor, the buying partner of London-based Sandra Cronan, who specializes in rare, unusual, and eclectic gems. It’s now old news that when Swift announced her engagement to Kansas City Chiefs star Travis Kelce in August 2025—with a bezel-set ring featuring a jaw-dropping antique diamond, designed by Kindred Lubeck of Artifex Fine Jewelry in New York —she sparked a surge in interest around narrative-steeped stones. “There was a huge spike in requests for antique elongated cushion-cut diamonds, and it was near impossible to source a comparable stone,” explains Lisa Levinson, head of UK at Natural Diamond Council. And Taylor Swift isn’t the only one. Whether drawn by increasing environmental and ethical concerns around the mining of diamonds and precious metals, or entranced by the craftsmanship, many are opting for a pre-loved engagement ring. Kate Middleton has a 12-carat sapphire ring that previously belonged to Princess Diana, while Natalie Portman’s engagement ring was made using an antique, conflict-free diamond set in recycled platinum and designed by jeweller Jamie Wolf. Devon Lee Carlson. Photo: Instagram (@devonleecarlson) Most recently, Devon Lee Carlson announced her engagement to Duke Nicholson, sporting an original antique natural diamond, three-stone ring. Set with three old mine cut diamonds mounted in yellow gold, it’s over 100 years old. Carlson sourced her ring via fine jewelry concierge The Tray , who found the piece with Los Angeles-based Platt Boutique , run by brother-sister team Larry and Natasha, who specialize in unique, eclectic, rare jewels. “We sourced Devon’s ring

If you fall into the latter camp, it’s understandable—color, material, silhouette, whether or not that couch you love will actually fit all your friends on movie night… these are suddenly topics you’ve got to untangle on your own when picking out new furniture. On top of that, there are quite literally hundreds of online retailers to choose from. A push in the right direction is going to save you lots of time (and money)—so we put together a compendium of all our go-to spots. Vogue ’s Guide to the Best Online Furniture Stores For Contemporary Minimalism: Maiden Home For Durable Investments: Arhaus For Trendy Finds: Lulu and Georgia For Kid- and Pet-Friendly Furnishings: Sundays For Modern Heirlooms: Sixpenny For Vintage Treasures: 1stDibs For Design MVPs: Design Within Reach For the Best Designer Collabs: West Elm For Mid-century Modern Simplicity: Article For Custom Everything: The Inside For Coastal Cottage Dreams: Serena & Lily Make sure to consider a few important things before checking out. Furniture shipping can be a doozy, so if you need something sooner than later, whittle down your options to places offering faster delivery than something made to order. (Big-box brands, like Crate & Barrel or West Elm, are good starting points.) Another important question: Who will be living with this new piece of furniture? We’re not talking humans—those with pets or toddlers will want to look into stain-resistant performance fabrics to ensure your investment has a long life ahead of it. Lastly, there’s no hiding that furniture has gotten more expensive. Payment plans are becoming more popular with big retailers, making it easier to fulfill your dream hom

title: Juliet Cap Veils Are the Romantic Bridal Trend Sweeping the Wedding Scene | Vogue
content: Juliet Cap Veils Are the Romantic Bridal Trend Sweeping the Wedding Scene | Vogue Skip to main content Your All Access Pass GET THE APP Search Account Menu Menu Menu FASHION RUNWAY SHOPPING BEAUTY CULTURE LIVING VOGUE BUSINESS TEEN VOGUE Wedding Inspiration Planning & Advice For Wedding Guests The Bridal Shop FASHION Expand Celebrity Style Street Style Models Designers Trends Sustainability All Fashion RUNWAY SHOPPING Chevron BEAUTY Expand Celebrity Beauty Hair Makeup Nails Wellness Skin All Beauty CULTURE Expand Opinion TV & Movies Music Arts Books News Sports All Culture LIVING Expand Homes Travel Food Parties Royals All Living WEDDINGS Expand Wedding Inspiration Planning & Advice For Wedding Guests The Bridal Shop All Weddings VOGUE BUSINESS Expand Technology Sustainability Fashion Retail Companies Beauty Events Member Benefits Executive Reports VIDEO PHOTOVOGUE MORE NEWSLETTERS PODCAST MAGAZINE PROJECTS VOGUE ARCHIVE TEEN VOGUE Search Search Contact Us About Vogue Weddings Juliet Cap Veils Are the Romantic Bridal Trend Sweeping the Wedding Scene By Rebecca Cope June 22, 2026 Photo: Getty Images Save Story Save this story Save Story Save this story When it comes to wedding fashion, many of today’s brides like to look to the past for inspiration. Whether it’s a minimalist slip dress inspired by Carolyn Bessette in the ’90s, or a full-skirted lace gown like the one worn by Grace Kelly in the ’50s, every decade has its own signature—and timeless—look. Case in point: the Juliet cap veil, which is making a comeback for 2026. Named after William Shakespeare’s famous heroine, the style is inspired by the delicate bead or pearl-encrusted caps worn by the Renaissance aristocracy to cover the back of their heads. It was popularized as a wedding accessory in the ’20s by flappers, who wore them either with or without a veil attached and often embroidered with real or faux flowers. In the years since, they have come in and out of fashion, with celebrity brides including Hollywood star Ingrid Bergman (1937), First Lady Jackie Kennedy (1953), model Kate Moss (2011), and pop star Lily Allen (2011) all wearing variations on the design. Kate Moss in John Galliano at her 2011 wedding to Jamie Hince. Photo: Getty Images A closer look at Kate’s Juliet cap veil. Photo: Getty Images Lara Burn-Heyer, owner of the Veil Atelier, has made several Juliet cap veils recently, and has noticed an increase in demand. “The brides who come to us are often looking for something that doesn’t quite exist yet,” she explains. “These brides are not necessarily rejecting tradition, but they are looking for ways to reinterpret it. Juliet caps sit slightly outside the mainstream bridal playbook. They’re romantic and evocative, but they also feel individual. For a certain type of bride, they offer an opportunity to express personality rather than simply participate in a tradition.” Lily Allen in Delphine Manivet in 2011. Photo: Getty Images Jackie Kennedy, then Jacqueline Lee Bouvier, in

title: 7 Highlights From Copenhagen’s 3 Days of Design | Vogue
content: 7 Highlights From Copenhagen’s 3 Days of Design | Vogue Skip to main content Your All Access Pass GET THE APP Search Account Menu Menu Menu FASHION RUNWAY SHOPPING BEAUTY CULTURE LIVING VOGUE BUSINESS TEEN VOGUE FASHION Expand Celebrity Style Street Style Models Designers Trends Sustainability All Fashion RUNWAY SHOPPING Chevron BEAUTY Expand Celebrity Beauty Hair Makeup Nails Wellness Skin All Beauty CULTURE Expand Opinion TV & Movies Music Arts Books News Sports All Culture LIVING Expand Homes Travel Food Parties Royals All Living WEDDINGS Expand Wedding Inspiration Planning & Advice For Wedding Guests The Bridal Shop All Weddings VOGUE BUSINESS Expand Technology Sustainability Fashion Retail Companies Beauty Events Member Benefits Executive Reports VIDEO PHOTOVOGUE MORE NEWSLETTERS PODCAST MAGAZINE PROJECTS VOGUE ARCHIVE TEEN VOGUE Search Search Contact Us About Vogue Homes 7 Highlights From Copenhagen’s 3 Days of Design By Hayley Maitland June 15, 2026 Tekla’s The Heart of Living, staged in partnership with architectural studio Mentze Ottenstein. Photo: Courtesy of Tekla Save Story Save this story Save Story Save this story Copenhagen in June is the city at its most alluring: Danes taking early-morning dips at Kalvebod Bølge; the sweet chestnuts in leaf along the cobbled avenues; and nearly 18 hours of daylight in which to linger in the Rosenborg Garden or browse for vintage finds at the loppemarkeder . Now in its 13th year, 3 Days of Design takes place against this midsummer backdrop, filling the city’s Renaissance and Rococo landmarks with the best in Scandinavian design, with more than 400 exhibitors popping up between Nordhavn and Islands Brygge from June 10 to 12. Like Copenhagen Fashion Week, hospitality is as central to 3 Days as the design itself, with much chatter this year about the first glimpse of Frederik Bille Brahe’s latest restaurant Daphne, while the fact that Tina Seidenfaden Busck is now letting her 18th-century gallery The Apartment for overnight stays was met with as much enthusiasm as her curation of Artists I Collect and Admire . As the 2026 edition of the fair draws to a close, Vogue recaps seven highlights. Georg Jensen said “game on” Photo: Courtesy of Georg Jensen Since taking the helm at Georg Jensen in 2024, creative director Paula Gerbase has set about infusing the 122-year-old silverware brand’s offering with a sense of play; recall her sterling-silver espresso cups and ice-cream spoons for Salone del Mobile 2025. For this year’s 3 Days of Design, the Danish house installed its own topiary-lined garden in the Højbro Plads to debut a series of lawn-game sets hand-carved from walnut in Copenhagen; picture kongespil batons adorned with silver renderings of Danish wildlife from the Georg Jensen smithy, along with a range of knick-knacks, from spinning tops to sterling yo-yos. Games were something of a theme at the 2026 fair; see, too, Central Saint Martins alumna Helle Mardahl’s Game On exhibition at the glass artis

title: This Cult-Favorite Lighting Design Studio Is Now Making Its Own Homewares | Vogue
content: This Cult-Favorite Lighting Design Studio Is Now Making Its Own Homewares | Vogue Skip to main content Your All Access Pass GET THE APP Search Account Menu Menu Menu FASHION RUNWAY SHOPPING BEAUTY CULTURE LIVING VOGUE BUSINESS TEEN VOGUE FASHION Expand Celebrity Style Street Style Models Designers Trends Sustainability All Fashion RUNWAY SHOPPING Chevron BEAUTY Expand Celebrity Beauty Hair Makeup Nails Wellness Skin All Beauty CULTURE Expand Opinion TV & Movies Music Arts Books News Sports All Culture LIVING Expand Homes Travel Food Parties Royals All Living WEDDINGS Expand Wedding Inspiration Planning & Advice For Wedding Guests The Bridal Shop All Weddings VOGUE BUSINESS Expand Technology Sustainability Fashion Retail Companies Beauty Events Member Benefits Executive Reports VIDEO PHOTOVOGUE MORE NEWSLETTERS PODCAST MAGAZINE PROJECTS VOGUE ARCHIVE TEEN VOGUE Search Search Contact Us About Vogue Homes This Cult-Favorite Lighting Design Studio Is Now Making Its Own Homewares By Nicole Kliest June 8, 2026 Photo: In Common With Save Story Save this story Save Story Save this story The great divide between industrial manufacturing and traditional craftsmanship is widely lamented among aesthetes. “It’s sort of like a TV dinner versus a home-cooked meal," Nick Ozemba muses. He and his In Common With co-founder Felicia Hung founded their design studio firmly in the home-cooked category, but with the caveat of scaling up production far more than you typically see with old-world craft. They also anchored the company around collaboration, using each collection as an opportunity to work with specialized ateliers around the world, such as ceramicists, glassblowers, and metalsmiths. A couple of years ago, Ozemba and Hung also opened a concept store, Quarters, in a 19th-century Tribeca loft as a way of seeing how their catalog sat in dialogue with other objects. The space is styled like a residence, and nearly everything—vintage furniture, independent works by fellow artists, In Common With collections—can be shopped. And though their design studio is known for its focus on lighting, In Common With’s latest release (titled Lido) signals an exciting expansion into making their own homewares. In Common With’s Tribeca concept store, Quarters. Photo: In Common With Ozemba and Hung founded In Common With in 2018, but initially met on their very first day of art school, where they both studied furniture design. “We naturally gravitated towards one another and knew we’d end up doing something together at some point,” Ozemba says. After graduating, Hung worked in manufacturing and design at several lighting and furniture studios, while Ozemba went into interior design. When the duo was ready to launch a business, they pivoted toward lighting, but with the intention of eventually expanding. “We’ve always thought of ourselves as a design studio rather than exclusively a lighting company,” Ozemba explains, “but lighting just happened to be our first category because of

This season, it’s all about the caftan. The perennial beach staple, known as (I hate to say it) a throw-on-and-go swimsuit cover-up , is returning in a way that feels more inspired by its past—while still nodding to the icons that embraced it. Vogue ’s Favorite Caftans: For Subtle Embellishment Sara Cristina pearl-embellished caftan Jump to review $550 NET-A-PORTER For Summer in the City Tory Burch lace-up caftan Jump to review $695 NET-A-PORTER The Printed Pick Alémais tie-detailed midi-dress Jump to review $690 NET-A-PORTER For Warm Late Nights Taller Marmo Cecil fringed caftan Jump to review $1,795 NET-A-PORTER For the Minimalist The Row Julen dress Jump to review $1,950 THE ROW For Something Short H&M Muslin beach caftan Jump to review $60 H&M From the rich textures worn by Marella Agnelli, the Italian socialite and Truman Capote swan , to the vivid color, print, and embroidery embraced by André Leon Talley, whose caftans could be seen at “ Superfine: Tailoring Black Style ” at the Met last year, designers are taking these past inspirations and bringing them into the present. Today’s caftans aren’t just to be worn over a bikini, but styled in unexpected ways that capture summer’s expressive, free-spirited tone. Our favorite contemporary interpretation? Worn as a dress over pants—a look Julianne Moore’s Sirens character Michaela Kell nails ; her wardrobe alone makes the limited series worth the watch. Whether in chiffon, silk, linen, or crisp cotton, a billowing caftan is an inherently versatile and effortless piece that brings comfort, ease, and elegance into a wardrobe, no matter the occasion. Ask any Vogue editor and the answer will be a resounding no . In fact, at the first sign of spring, leather carryalls get swiftly replaced by a rotating cast of small and large woven shoppers. (The summer season is short as is—why save its best pieces for only two weeks a year?) And one label in particular makes some of our favorite ones: Hunting Season. Bogotá-based founder and creative director, Danielle Corona, a Florida native with Cuban heritage, works with expert artisans in Colombia who thoughtfully handcraft everything from accessories to leather home goods. From the striped canvas totes to the satin clutches and tassel-adorned evening bags, an elegant, carefree ease imbues these designs—all brought to life in a soothing, natural color palette honoring earth’s bounty. In love? So are we—and for a limited time, you can save 15% off sitewide as an Insider. Backyard weddings are the ultimate way to celebrate love in a way that feels more personal. For guests , however, the dress code can feel like a grey area—it’s inherently low-key, but not necessarily casual. Vogue’s Guide to What to Wear to a Backyard Wedding The Pleated Midi Fforme Lumie satin midi dress Jump to review $1,695 NET-A-PORTER The Handy Accessory H&M folding fan Jump to review $13 H&M The Silk Mini Dôen Maltina mini dress Jump to review $300 NET-A-PORTER The Raffia Clutch Bottega Veneta Andiamo clutch Jump to review $3,300 BOTTEGA VENETA The Practical Shoe Manolo Blahnik Ballerimu square-toe mules Jump to review $835 NORDSTROM The Floral Print Ulla Johnson Clove maxi dress Jump to review $760 NET-A-PORTER The Lace-Up Espadrilles Castañer Carina pumps Jump to review $200 BLOOMINGDALE'S When in doubt, lean into a bit of romance with flowing silhouettes and subtle prints—a sweet floral is always a safe bet for a garden ceremony . The main thing you have to pay attention to? Your shoes. Prepare for grassy terrain with a shoe that won’t sink into the ground; skip the sky-high stiletto for a wedge espadrille or low kitten heel. In the accessories department, a hand fan for balmy weather or a pair of sunglasses for daytime nuptials make for simple but practical finishing touches. Ahead, seven backyard wedding outfit ideas for your next guest appearance. Coming Up Roses Match your dress to the setting with this swishy floral midi dress from Ulla Johnson. Discreet but noticeable, luxury but not overpriced, Talia Loubaton has perfected the art of the jacket (and since her launch in 2022 many other things),” Page says. “If there is one thing you buy from this brand, let it be that, or in my case, don't by another–I currently have four in my rotation and have lost count of the many ways I have worn and styled them–with denim for a dinner with friends to a black kick-flare pant for the office. I put them on and immediately feel complete!” Senior shopping editor Talia Abbas feels similarly about her Liberowe jackets, which are all handmade in London from luxurious, responsibly-sourced fabrics, including deadstock. “There is no jacket more special or cherished in my wardrobe right now,” she says. “A perfect little black jacket, the Cora , is on my wish list right now.” Styling-wise, Abbas says she “likes to balance out their femininity with something totally unexpected—as I believe Talia intended—like faded blue jeans and leather flip-flops.” Step inside Talia Loubaton’s Liberowe, where each piece bridges past and present with her signature feminine meets masculine spin. Delicate dinner jackets find their match in mini hemlines and bare legs; ruffled cashmere cardigans soften wide-leg trousers, and floor-length tailored coats elevate the casual ease of jeans and a button-down. Vogue Shopping Insiders can enjoy any of these editor-loved pieces for 15% off, now through June 31. title: 9 Vogue Editor-Approved Summer Travel Outfits to Jet-Set in Style | Vogue
content: 9 Vogue Editor-Approved Summer Travel Outfits to Jet-Set in Style | Vogue Skip to main content Your All Access Pass GET THE APP Search Account Menu Menu Menu FASHION RUNWAY SHOPPING BEAUTY CULTURE LIVING VOGUE BUSINESS TEEN VOGUE New Arrivals Summer in the City The Vacation Shop Under $300 Shoes Bags Editor Wish Lists FASHION Expand Celebrity Style Street Style Models Designers Trends Sustainability All Fashion RUNWAY SHOPPING Chevron BEAUTY Expand Celebrity Beauty Hair Makeup Nails Wellness Skin All Beauty CULTURE Expand Opinion TV & Movies Music Arts Books News Sports All Culture LIVING Expand Homes Travel Food Parties Royals All Living WEDDINGS Expand Wedding Inspiration Planning & Advice For Wedding Guests The Bridal Shop All Weddings VOGUE BUSINESS Expand Technology Sustainability Fashion Retail Companies Beauty Events Member Benefits Executive Reports VIDEO PHOTOVOGUE MORE NEWSLETTERS PODCAST MAGAZINE PROJECTS VOGUE ARCHIVE TEEN VOGUE Search Search Contact Us About Vogue Shopping What Vogue Editors Are Wearing to Travel This Summer By Elly Leavitt June 24, 2026 Photo: Courtesy of Talia Abbas Save Story Save this story Save Story Save this story Where are you going this summer ? This season is seeing team Vogue scatter from downtown Manhattan to all corners of the globe—from the Greek islands to the West Coast, from the Balearics to Zanzibar, suffice it to say our itineraries have little in common. The travel outfits , though, are another story. Curious to see how our editors balance freezing plane cabins with balmy destinations, we polled the team; and it looks like there’s one item in particular that’s a favorite across the board. title: 9-5: How Maria McManus Layers Feminine Staples With Sharp Tailoring | Vogue
content: 9-5: How Maria McManus Layers Feminine Staples With Sharp Tailoring | Vogue Skip to main content Your All Access Pass GET THE APP Search Account Menu Menu Menu FASHION RUNWAY SHOPPING BEAUTY CULTURE LIVING VOGUE BUSINESS TEEN VOGUE New Arrivals Summer in the City The Vacation Shop Under $300 Shoes Bags Editor Wish Lists FASHION Expand Celebrity Style Street Style Models Designers Trends Sustainability All Fashion RUNWAY SHOPPING Chevron BEAUTY Expand Celebrity Beauty Hair Makeup Nails Wellness Skin All Beauty CULTURE Expand Opinion TV & Movies Music Arts Books News Sports All Culture LIVING Expand Homes Travel Food Parties Royals All Living WEDDINGS Expand Wedding Inspiration Planning & Advice For Wedding Guests The Bridal Shop All Weddings VOGUE BUSINESS Expand Technology Sustainability Fashion Retail Companies Beauty Events Member Benefits Executive Reports VIDEO PHOTOVOGUE MORE NEWSLETTERS PODCAST MAGAZINE PROJECTS VOGUE ARCHIVE TEEN VOGUE Search Search Contact Us About Vogue Fashion 9-5: How Maria McManus Layers Feminine Staples With Sharp Tailoring By Talia Abbas June 5, 2026 Save Story Save this story Save Story Save this story Piecing together an outfit comes naturally to Maria McManus, the Irish-born designer known for her sustainable approach to wardrobing. “I love two or three-piece dressing because I can put more ideas together,” she tells me on our Zoom call from her Tribeca apartment, breaking down her look of the day: a white T-shirt, navy jumper, ivory silk trousers, and a pearl choker from Sophie Buhai. McManus, who calls New York City home but travels extensively, has come to appreciate the structure of a uniform and a refined color palette. “When you’re constantly thinking about dressing other people, sometimes you just want it to be really easy with your own clothing,” she says. She relies on a few constants from her own collections, mixing them with accessories from old Celine, The Row, Alaïa, Carven, and Lemaire. Tailoring is one of her style signifiers; it’s come to define her work as a designer, crafting high-quality clothing meant to stand the test of time. On the weekdays, you’ll often find McManus in some sort of blazer and trouser combination; if it’s a summer Friday, she might swap in her pleated pants for a drawstring sateen pair or a loose linen, depending on whether she’s busy in the city or unwinding at her home in Montauk. Materials play a central role in both McManus’s creative process and her personal wardrobe. In the five years since launching her brand, she’s thought carefully about fabric, to the point that she can identify the composition of any given piece she’s designed—or has on. The navy sweater she’s in today, for instance, is made of recycled cashmere with organic cotton from a mill in Italy. She mostly wears organic materials—wool, satin, silk, and organic cotton—but welcomes “little hints of lace or macramé to break things up.” Splitting her time between Manhattan, Montauk, and Ireland, McManus share

Its range of sensible suede totes and shoulder bags make for perfect everyday styles, while its smaller designs are equally covetable for moments when you don’t have to transport laptop and lunch to and from work. New to the lineup but no less desirable: the Belted Buckle Bag (defined by its long, loop-like closure and available in multiple strap lengths) and the Flat Leather Basket Bag —a summer must-have perfect for adding personality to with a beaded bag charm. Liffner’s contemporary luxuries are already well-priced, which makes owning one of our editors’ favorites even more inviting as an Insider with 15% off. Since the Brazilian brand launched in 1962, its simple rubber sandals have become a warm-weather staple, beloved by celebrities and the fashion set for their easygoing nature. In a summer when flip-flops are having a major fashion moment, Havaianas reaffirm their enduring appeal. After all, you can’t go wrong with the classics. title: St. Agni Is the Minimalist Brand Kendall Jenner Can’t Get Enough Of | Vogue
content: St. Agni Is the Minimalist Brand Kendall Jenner Can’t Get Enough Of | Vogue Skip to main content Your All Access Pass GET THE APP Search Account Menu Menu Menu FASHION RUNWAY SHOPPING BEAUTY CULTURE LIVING VOGUE BUSINESS TEEN VOGUE FASHION Expand Celebrity Style Street Style Models Designers Trends Sustainability All Fashion RUNWAY SHOPPING Chevron BEAUTY Expand Celebrity Beauty Hair Makeup Nails Wellness Skin All Beauty CULTURE Expand Opinion TV & Movies Music Arts Books News Sports All Culture LIVING Expand Homes Travel Food Parties Royals All Living WEDDINGS Expand Wedding Inspiration Planning & Advice For Wedding Guests The Bridal Shop All Weddings VOGUE BUSINESS Expand Technology Sustainability Fashion Retail Companies Beauty Events Member Benefits Executive Reports VIDEO PHOTOVOGUE MORE NEWSLETTERS PODCAST MAGAZINE PROJECTS VOGUE ARCHIVE TEEN VOGUE Search Search Contact Us About Vogue Celebrity Style The Minimalist Brand Kendall Jenner Can’t Get Enough Of By Erika Veurink July 21, 2023 Kendall Jenner in a St. Agni dress. Photo: FRAT Save Story Save this story Save Story Save this story St. Agni designer and founder Lara Fells starts with one foundational question. “Do I need this item, and how would I want to use it?” This philosophy, alongside an architectural fervor for balance and clean lines, guides Fells season after season. The aesthetic is somewhere between airy and grounded—a hidden cutout plus a sheer overlay. Worn by celebrities like Kendall Jenner and Emma Stone, the Australian label is just as at home on the streets of Los Angeles as it is in its birthplace, Byron Bay. Before Byron Bay, there was a chance encounter between Fells and her romantic and business partner, Matt. Introduced by friends in their early 20s, the couple spent years traveling between Australia and the UK before settling down. “We felt drawn to the coastal-town lifestyle of Byron Bay, where we decided to establish our roots. It provided stability while nurturing our entrepreneurial dreams,” Fells says. She recalls the naivete that accompanied the couple’s decision to start St. Agni as a side hustle and how it ushered in “an openness to learning, adaptability, and the courage to venture into the unknown, which allowed us to navigate the path and grow organically.” Jenner wearing St. Agni at Coachella Photo: Nick Wiesner/Courtesy of Revolve More than minimalist, St. Agni has been a celebration of form and function since its inception. “We believe that there is inherent beauty in stripping away unnecessary details and focusing on essential elements,” notes Fells. There’s an equal measure of elegance and ease in every design. The brand’s formfitting dresses, a favorite of Jenner’s for Coachella or a trip to Paris, are a perfect case study. While trends, like cutouts and sheer suiting, are alluded to, they’re never the determining factor. Save to wishlist Save to wishlist St. Agni cutout cotton tank top $100 LUISA VIA ROMA $99 ST. I think that's a very Australian way of dressing—not too done up, just effortless.” It’s little surprise that since founding her label in 2016, countless women have sought to recreate her signature minimalist polish. Already a go-to for fashion insiders, it was Sofia Richie Grainge that really put the brand on the map after she was spotted in the Emma vest and skirt set during her Côte d’Azur wedding weekend in 2023. Meghan Markle, Katie Holmes, and Selena Gomez have all been spotted in her designs, too. Beyond the label’s beloved sets, Posse’s timeless take color and romantic print (polka dot! gingham! floral!) will bring a burst of feel-good energy to your warm-weather wardrobe. Round out your summer capsule with Vogue -loved brand as an Insider with 20% off your first order. title: 9-5: Posse’s Danielle Mulham Shares Her Secret for Effortless Dressing | Vogue
content: 9-5: Posse’s Danielle Mulham Shares Her Secret for Effortless Dressing | Vogue Skip to main content Your All Access Pass GET THE APP Search Account Menu Menu Menu FASHION RUNWAY SHOPPING BEAUTY CULTURE LIVING VOGUE BUSINESS TEEN VOGUE New Arrivals Summer in the City The Vacation Shop Under $300 Shoes Bags Editor Wish Lists FASHION Expand Celebrity Style Street Style Models Designers Trends Sustainability All Fashion RUNWAY SHOPPING Chevron BEAUTY Expand Celebrity Beauty Hair Makeup Nails Wellness Skin All Beauty CULTURE Expand Opinion TV & Movies Music Arts Books News Sports All Culture LIVING Expand Homes Travel Food Parties Royals All Living WEDDINGS Expand Wedding Inspiration Planning & Advice For Wedding Guests The Bridal Shop All Weddings VOGUE BUSINESS Expand Technology Sustainability Fashion Retail Companies Beauty Events Member Benefits Executive Reports VIDEO PHOTOVOGUE MORE NEWSLETTERS PODCAST MAGAZINE PROJECTS VOGUE ARCHIVE TEEN VOGUE Search Search Contact Us About Vogue Fashion 9-5: Posse’s Danielle Mulham Shares Her Secret for Effortless Dressing By Maia Torres March 8, 2024 Photos: Courtesy of Lucy Blake Save Story Save this story Save Story Save this story Danielle Mulham knows a thing or two about quiet luxury . In fact, she’s partly responsible for its mainstream movement. “As soon as I heard about the trend, I felt like we had been doing that all along, but not noticing that was what it was,” Mulham, swirling a glass of iced tea on Zoom, tells Vogue. “Our brand definitely epitomizes all facets of quiet luxury. It's an accessible trend, and I think everyone looks good in it.” About a year ago, the Aussie founder and creative director of the linen leisurewear label, Posse , saw a surge in demand for the brand’s Emma vest and skirt set—all thanks to It-girl Sofia Richie Grainge who sported the crisp white linen ensemble during her Cote d’Azur wedding weekend . “I’ve never seen exposure like that before,” she recalls. “Seeing the support from people and just looking back on our wholesome early days to where we are now, is just amazing.” Months later, several more celebrities began to follow suit. Meghan Markle and Katie Holmes were spotted sporting the brand’s Theo dress, while the likes of Lily Aldrige and Selena Gomez have also added Posse pieces into their wardrobes. As a teenager, Mulham would set aside money from her allowance to buy international magazines that were hard to come by in Sydney. She flipped through editorials styled by Grace Coddington and Edward Enninful, which crystallized her calling in fashion styling. “Back then I thought you could be either a fashion designer or an editor, until I saw the words ‘styled by,’ and I was like, that! That’s what I want to do!” In college, Mulham pursued a degree in creative direction at the Whitehouse Institute of Design, a “very niche program” she calls, where she collaborated alongside photography students, graphic illustrators, and aspiring fashion designers. Then, while on a

title: Every Famous Bride Who Wore Chanel on Her Wedding Day | Vogue
content: Every Famous Bride Who Wore Chanel on Her Wedding Day | Vogue Skip to main content Your All Access Pass GET THE APP Search Account Menu Menu Menu FASHION RUNWAY SHOPPING BEAUTY CULTURE LIVING VOGUE BUSINESS TEEN VOGUE Wedding Inspiration Planning & Advice For Wedding Guests The Bridal Shop FASHION Expand Celebrity Style Street Style Models Designers Trends Sustainability All Fashion RUNWAY SHOPPING Chevron BEAUTY Expand Celebrity Beauty Hair Makeup Nails Wellness Skin All Beauty CULTURE Expand Opinion TV & Movies Music Arts Books News Sports All Culture LIVING Expand Homes Travel Food Parties Royals All Living WEDDINGS Expand Wedding Inspiration Planning & Advice For Wedding Guests The Bridal Shop All Weddings VOGUE BUSINESS Expand Technology Sustainability Fashion Retail Companies Beauty Events Member Benefits Executive Reports VIDEO PHOTOVOGUE MORE NEWSLETTERS PODCAST MAGAZINE PROJECTS VOGUE ARCHIVE TEEN VOGUE Search Search Contact Us About Vogue Weddings Every Famous Bride Who Wore Chanel on Her Wedding Day By Rebecca Cope June 23, 2026 Photo: Courtesy of Dua Lipa Save Story Save this story Save Story Save this story cAfter what felt like a lifetime of waiting, pop star Dua Lipa finally unveiled her wedding dress this weekend. Despite speculation that it would be designed by one of her close friends, Simon Porte Jacquemus or Donatella Versace, it was actually Chanel who had the honour, with the gown being Matthieu Blazy’s first bespoke wedding dress since joining the house. Featuring a backless design and a mermaid-shaped skirt, plus elaborate beading and bejewelled drapery at the base of the back, it’s sure to go down in fashion history alongside other well-known Chanel wedding dresses. Dua is now part of an exclusive club of brides, including these famous women. Sofia Richie Grainge Sofia Richie Grainge wore three different Chanel dresses across her wedding weekend in 2023, all designed by then creative director, Virginie Viard. For her rehearsal dinner, she wore a high-neck, long-sleeve beaded gown. Then, for her wedding day, she first wore a floor-length, criss-cross neck dress, which featured floral and heart embroidery in ivory sequins. Finally, she changed into a structured mini, which featured the house’s signature camellia on it. Speaking to Vogue at the time, she explained: “I always had this vision of the big princess dress, and going big, but as I got older, I realised that I wanted something a bit more simple… maybe ‘simple’ isn’t the right word, because my dress definitely is not simple!” Sofia Richie Grainge. Photo: Norman & Blake Keira Knightley As the face of the brand’s Chanel Coco Mademoiselle fragrance since 2007, it was a no-brainer for actor Keira Knightley to choose the brand for her South of France wedding to The Klaxons frontman James Righton back in 2013. Eschewing tradition, she opted for a simple Chanel mini dress with a tulle skirt that she had worn previously, accessorising with a floral head garland, a Chanel woo

title: Taylor Swift Ditches Her Dresses In Favor of Boho Denim and Platforms | Vogue
content: Taylor Swift Ditches Her Dresses In Favor of Boho Denim and Platforms | Vogue Skip to main content Your All Access Pass GET THE APP Search Account Menu Menu Menu FASHION RUNWAY SHOPPING BEAUTY CULTURE LIVING VOGUE BUSINESS TEEN VOGUE FASHION Expand Celebrity Style Street Style Models Designers Trends Sustainability All Fashion RUNWAY SHOPPING Chevron BEAUTY Expand Celebrity Beauty Hair Makeup Nails Wellness Skin All Beauty CULTURE Expand Opinion TV & Movies Music Arts Books News Sports All Culture LIVING Expand Homes Travel Food Parties Royals All Living WEDDINGS Expand Wedding Inspiration Planning & Advice For Wedding Guests The Bridal Shop All Weddings VOGUE BUSINESS Expand Technology Sustainability Fashion Retail Companies Beauty Events Member Benefits Executive Reports VIDEO PHOTOVOGUE MORE NEWSLETTERS PODCAST MAGAZINE PROJECTS VOGUE ARCHIVE TEEN VOGUE Search Search Contact Us About Vogue Celebrity Style Taylor Swift Ditches Her Dresses In Favor of Boho Denim and Platforms By Olivia Allen June 16, 2026 Photo: Getty Images Save Story Save this story Save Story Save this story In terms of celebrity weddings, they don’t get much bigger than Taylor Swift ’s forthcoming nuptials. Dua Lipa and Callum Turner did themselves proud in Sicily, but the official union of your English teacher and your gym teacher—rumored to be taking place over the same weekend as America’s 250th Independence Day—will arguably have more cultural impact than the Fourth of July itself. Speculation has naturally turned to the dress. Will Swift go with classic Vivienne Westwood , custom Givenchy (currently the front runner) or, judging by her recent wardrobe choices, lean more boho with her bridal style? Despite being in the final stages of planning, the singer is still finding time to… well, sing. She was spotted heading to Electric Lady Studios in New York this week wearing a slouchy Chloé Penelope shoulder bag and Gucci Horsebit platform sandals that further emphasized the nostalgic, ’70s vibe of her front-pocket jeans. Photo: Getty Images Taylor’s chunky Gucci stompers are a new addition to her shoe collection (though she’s a longtime fan of a granny sandal ), and her combination of denim and a short-sleeved knit was more laid-back than the outfits we’re used to seeing from the star. Recent looks have included Old Hollywood-esque custom Givenchy, off-the-shoulder Erdem and sparkling Oscar de la Renta. Even if she is enjoying dabbling in Dakota-coded daytime dressing, it seems unlikely that Taylor will plump for crochet or fringe on her wedding day. But could this bohemian streak hint at a more retro sound for her next album? Time (and the online sleuths of the Swiftie fandom), will tell. The Park Is the Essentials Brand You Need on Your Radar By Vogue June 6, 2025 Save Story Save this story Save Story Save this story Photo: Courtesy of The Park The Park was founded by creative director and public relations veteran Sarah Bonello with a single aim: to make getting dressed in the morning easy . It’s a simple premise that’s surprisingly challenging, and an all too familiar one at that. (An overflowing closet full of nothing? We’ve all been there.) Streamlining your wardrobe all starts with the foundations—ultra-comfortable essentials that can be effortlessly dressed up or down. And for the ultimate capsule, The Park’s luxurious base layers should be your go-to. The brand’s tightly-edited line features no more, no less than the building blocks of a tight, hardworking edit: an everyday bralette and layerable boy short; a figure-hugging mini and maxi and a handful of sleek bodysuits, tops, and leggings all made from the brand’s signature biodegradable stretch-knit blend (a mix of super-soft MicroTencel and recycled elastane) and in black and nude neutrals. The next time you’re looking for new basics—consider The Park. Executive shopping director Libby Page has styled its Toby Boy Short with a Khaite jacket for Vogue ’s Pre-Met party, while senior shopping editor Talia Abbas loves the Heather Tee for off-duty and traveling. Plus, as a Vogue Shopping Insider, you can save 15% off for a limited time on your first order. These retro-futuristic frames not only do a great job of blocking the sun, but their wraparound look also gives a stylish mystique that’s sporty and edgy at once. The one caveat? They can be a little intimidating to wear. Vogue ’s Favorite Shield Sunglasses The Emerging Label to Know Thistles The Atlas sunglasses Jump to review $395 THISTLES The Under-$150 Pick Jimmy Fairly The Aela sunglasses Jump to review $148 JIMMY FAIRLY The Runway Find Miu Miu Ivresse sunglasses Jump to review $440 MIU MIU The Ultra-Sporty Shield Loewe speed shield sunglasses Jump to review $590 LOEWE The Aviator Style Celine aviator-style sunglasses Jump to review $490 NET-A-PORTER The Full-On Wraparound Saint Laurent SL 914 sunglasses Jump to review $650 SAINT LAURENT To get a better sense of their real-life potential, our team decided to put a few of the season’s trendiest styles to the test, trying on pairs from Celine to Khaite and Thistles (a homegrown New York brand that can’t keep its best-selling Atlas in stock). Some shades we tried came straight from the runway (see: Tory Burch’s runway wrap and Miu Miu’s Ivresse); others already had a place in editors’ handbags. Our fashion market director, Maddy Fass, was keen to try her Khaite x Oliver Peoples pair in another color. Ditto for Irene Kim, our production and editorial associate, who is in possession of not one but two of the season’s bestsellers—lucky girl! As for my colleagues trying them on for size for the first time? Turns out they were pleasantly surprised by the general fit; our beauty shopping editor, Kiana Murden, was quickly won over by Tom Ford’s pair. title: The Best Sneakers to Wear With Dresses: 25+ Chic Outfit Ideas | Vogue

Whether you’re looking to fill your closet with easy ensembles to beat the heat or packing for an upcoming getaway , start filling your cart with the best dresses of summer —which just so happen to complement all the incredibly cool sneakers we’ve been eying lately. Consider the best sneakers to wear with summer dresses as your unstoppable power pair for the months ahead. Vogue’s Summer Dress Wish List The Cotton Mini Dôen Maltina mini dress Jump to review $298 NORDSTROM The Easy Canvas Keds Champion sneakers Jump to review $55 NORDSTROM The Pretty Polka Dot Dôen Safia maxi dress Jump to review $450 NET-A-PORTER The Classic Black Superga 2750 Cotu Classic sneakers Jump to review $75 NORDSTROM The Red Maxi Matteau shirred maxi dress Jump to review $570 NET-A-PORTER The Editor-Favorite Adidas x Wales Bonner Karintha sneakers Jump to review $220 NET-A-PORTER The Embroidered Sundress Staud Mariella dress Jump to review $325 NET-A-PORTER The Suede Runner Dries Van Noten Dustin slim sneakers Jump to review $630 NORDSTROM Celebrities like Gigi Hadid, Dakota Johnson, and Rihanna are regularly sporting this simple outfit idea on the street—giving us ample inspiration to do the same. It’s a laidback, East-meets-West coast way of dressing that emphasizes comfort with good taste, making this combination undeniably appealing. Pro tip: It also doesn’t hurt to have a trending sneaker in the mix, to amp up something as minimal as a slinky slip or little black dress , as these women so often prove. Whether you’re eying a soft jersey T-shirt dress or have a printed poplin maxi in mind for an upcoming vacation, be sure to add a pair of cool coordinating sneakers to match.
